outcomes.text: You do the far loop yourself, and the last crawler leg catches you rationing air like an optimist, so the count costs you a day in the infirmary with a compression headache. The vote still comes back a wall. The ore hauler delivers it to the adjustor personally, at her full and considerable rest weight, and reports that he took a step back, which the row has framed.
effects: schedule 4 r18_moon_landlord_reversal Rent comes due on the shadows again

choices.text: Strike perpetuity and send the folder back

success.text: You return their folder with one edit: the word perpetual struck, and above it, for so long as the landlord provides gravity at grade, initialed. Head office signs, confident that gravity provides itself. It does; the question of who exactly provides it is now load-bearing, and the row intends to stand on that question at its full combined weight.
effects: schedule 4 r18_moon_landlord_reversal Rent comes due on the shadows again

fail.text: Your edit comes back countersigned but altered again, because the last hand on a document wins, and the adjustor's hand added whichever is longer to your clause. You have signed a rate that outlives the title, the crater, and arguably the sun. The clerk files it under lessons. The warden logs the day as weather: clear, no survivors.
effects: schedule 4 r18_moon_landlord_reversal Rent comes due on the shadows again

text:
MOON LANDLORD

Dome Nine calls the board into session, and the ore hauler stands up with a single sheet, handwritten, ruled with a wrench for a straightedge. It is an invoice to Lunar Shelter Assets for mass provision services: one tenant, sixty-two years continuous, supplying the gravitational amenity by remaining heavy, never once absent, rate per kilogram per year, plus interest. The paperwork requires a name at last, and she gives it: Magda Stone. The lease is unfolded to answer, and for the first time on record, Schedule G says nothing.

choices.text: Chip in so every tenant files their weight

outcomes.text: Three coins covers your filing and a stack of blank forms, and by night shift the whole rim is invoicing: forty domes of mass provision services, retroactive to birth, the same trick Rider K taught them, pointed home. The board processes the stack with ceremonial slowness. Schedule G, served with all of it at once, warms its paper anxiously and reaches no conclusion. Somewhere at head office a total is being calculated, and it compounds.
effects: schedule 5 r18_moon_landlord_legacy Rent comes due on the shadows again

effects: schedule 5 r18_moon_landlord_legacy Rent comes due on the shadows again

outcomes.text: The form queue in the window room turns into a scrum over who has been heavy longest, and an ice cutter's clipboard catches you above the eye, slowly, with time to read it coming. The invoices get filed anyway, dented. Yours goes in with a thumbprint of blood on it, which the clerk notes as a seal, acceptable.
effects: schedule 5 r18_moon_landlord_legacy Rent comes due on the shadows again

choices.text: Submit her invoice as a test case

outcomes.text: The board hears the test case in the window room with the window ignored for once. Lunar Shelter Assets argues that gravity is provided by the moon; the reply, read flatly by the clerk, is that the moon is not a party to the lease and has submitted no invoice, whereas Magda Stone has. Under its own definitions, an amenity has a supplier, and the supplier is owed. Schedule G concedes in writing, which is the only way it knows how to weep.
effects: schedule 5 r18_moon_landlord_legacy Rent comes due on the shadows again

outcomes.text: Head office answers the test case with a motion to define tenant mass as landlord inventory while on the premises, which would make Magda Stone a fixture. The board takes it under review instead of laughing, which is the frightening part. The row spends a week legally unsure whether it owns its own weight. The clerk files the motion under NO, but the folder is thin.
effects: schedule 5 r18_moon_landlord_legacy Rent comes due on the shadows again

choices.text: Sell your gravity claim for flat coin

outcomes.text: The adjustor offers fourteen coins, flat, for your mass provision claim: sign here, assign the rights, walk away light. You sign. Whatever the claim becomes, it becomes it for Lunar Shelter Assets now, and in the corridor the ore hauler weighs you with a look and finds the reading low. You are the first tenant on the rim to sell your own heaviness back to the landlord, and the invoice you might have been is filed under theirs.
effects: schedule 5 r18_moon_landlord_legacy Rent comes due on the shadows again

text:
MOON LANDLORD

Settlement day is held in the window room, doors open, kettle going. Lunar Shelter Assets has conceded the amenity: gravity is reclassified as tenant supplied, shadows revert to natural encumbrance, and the arrears run the other way, paid out in dome repairs and a decade of zero rent. Schedule G, decommissioned, has been granted one final posting. It lies on the board's table holding the minutes down, a paperweight, which on the moon is a genuinely difficult job, and the first useful thing it has ever done with weight.

choices.text: Draft the new lease in forty plain words

success.text: You write it in one sitting: domes hold their own paper, air is nobody's product, disputes go to the window room, tea is implied. Forty words, no schedule, no appendix, nowhere for anything to nest. The board adopts it by show of hands and one raised kettle. Schedule G, from its post on the table, keeps the new lease flat while the ink dries, gently, like an old dog with a newborn.

fail.text: Your forty words survive committee as ninety, then a hundred and forty, because everyone on the rim has one clause they cannot live without, and the clauses breed in the drawer overnight. The clerk swears the drawer was locked. It is adopted anyway, warm to the touch. The warden logs it under weather: familiar.

choices.text: Sell your annotated Schedule G

outcomes.text: A collector from the far side offers fourteen coins for your annotated copy of Schedule G, margins and all, calling it an important document of the period. You sell your marginal notes, your grandmother clause citations, the lot. The copy leaves the crater under glass, bound for a paying audience. You take the seat and inherit the board's oldest tradition, the window room's jammed vent, which greets you with a faceful of scalding condensate on your first shift. The clerk hands you the maintenance log; it goes back thirty years and every entry is the same entry. You write yours, rub your cheek, and put the kettle on. The moon outside stays level and gray, holding the whole arrangement down, still free of charge.

PARADE ROUTE

Years seem to pass. the neighborhood defense choir stands beside the public avenue and counts what survived. The institutions are imperfect, the loudest hero is already writing a memoir, and fascists denied both power and good staging is finally possible if people keep doing the unglamorous work.

text:
DESALINATION

Tuesday, low tide, a BlueGold intern staples fresh lamination to the seawall: Schedule R, a tariff on unauthorized rain, effective retroactively to the last recorded cloud. The drought is in its third year; the ocean, fully privatized, glitters behind chain link with a customer service number on it. The widow who chairs the coast's water council reads the notice twice, then measures the morning's dew into a jam jar in case it needs declaring. She would like the desalination pumps switched on and the invoices to stop being surreal, in that order.

choices.text: Carry buckets with the seawall crew

success.text: You fall in beside the bucket line before anyone asks your name, which around here counts as a formal introduction. The widow assigns you a rota slot and a jam jar, and the crew adjusts its rhythm to include you without discussing it. On the seawall a young BlueGold meter reader watches the buckets go by and does not write a single one of them down, which is either negligence or the beginning of a personality.
effects: schedule 2 r18_desalination_assembly Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

fail.text: You take the heaviest bucket to make an impression, and the impression you make is on the seawall, spine first. The crew hauls you up, logs your injury in the accident book under acts of enthusiasm, and keeps you anyway. Somewhere overhead the first unauthorized cloud in months drifts past, unmetered, and the whole street feels briefly criminal.
effects: schedule 2 r18_desalination_assembly Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Contest Schedule R in writing

outcomes.text: Your objection cites the founding charter of the harbor, which predates BlueGold, the drought, and arguably punctuation. A junior clerk stamps it received with visible relief, as if someone had finally addressed the building in its native tongue. Schedule R gains a footnote suspending penalties pending review, and the widow calls that the first weather forecast in years worth believing.
effects: schedule 2 r18_desalination_assembly Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

outcomes.text: Your objection comes back annotated in four colors of ink, none of them encouraging. The tariff, it turns out, defines rain as any descending moisture including tears, which you then produced on the premises without a permit. The council thanks you for confirming what the fine print is capable of, and files your paperwork under evidence.
effects: schedule 2 r18_desalination_assembly Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Sign on as a freelance rain spotter for BlueGold

outcomes.text: BlueGold pays a spotter's fee for every unauthorized cloud reported, and you have excellent eyes. Fourteen coins arrive with a laminated badge and a lanyard reading WEATHER INTEGRITY PARTNER. On the seawall the bucket crew stops talking when you pass, which is its own kind of invoice.

text:
DESALINATION

Thursday evening the water council convenes in the empty fish market, under a ceiling still hopeful about fish. The agenda has one item, Schedule R, and the tariff itself attends in laminated form, propped on a chair like a difficult relative. The off duty meter reader sits at the back taking no notes, a personal best. The widow gavels with a pump valve and asks for ideas that are not arson.

choices.text: Read the tariff aloud as dramatic verse

outcomes.text: You perform Schedule R from the fish counter, clause by clause, with pauses. By subsection four, unauthorized dew, the room is weeping laughing; by subsection nine, ambient mist, so is the meter reader. A transcript circulates by morning, and BlueGold's regional office requests in writing that its own tariff no longer be quoted accurately in public. The clerk who drafted subsection nine reportedly asks to be transferred somewhere his work will not be read.
effects: schedule 3 r18_desalination_retaliation Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

outcomes.text: You perform Schedule R as tragedy and misjudge the room, which has been living it. The laughs die around subsection six, standing water, because half the audience has been fined for exactly that. The meeting ends early, and BlueGold's newsletter quotes you as a celebrity endorsement of the tariff, lightly edited, entirely smiling.
effects: schedule 3 r18_desalination_retaliation Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Sell the meeting minutes to BlueGold's regional office

outcomes.text: The regional director of rain pays handsomely for a full transcript, names attached. You annotate generously, invent a few tempers, and collect fourteen coins plus a promise of consideration for future consideration. The minutes get three neighbors reclassified as weather risks, which is not your department, which is the whole point of departments.

choices.text: Draw up the shared bucket rota yourself

success.text: You chart every roof, gutter, and able back on the coast into one rota with substitutions, so nobody hauls alone twice. The widow initials it, the chapel lends its bell for shift changes, and the document goes up where the tariff used to hang, which everyone pretends is a coincidence. Forty tired neighbors are not an army, but the buckets start arriving on time, and the lamination on the seawall develops its first curl of doubt.
effects: schedule 3 r18_desalination_retaliation Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

fail.text: You pin the rota too close to the tide line and spend the night rescuing it, wet to the ribs. A BlueGold patrol logs you for unlicensed collection of seawater by trouser. The rota survives, smeared but legible, and the crew decides anyone that stubborn about paperwork belongs to them now.
effects: schedule 3 r18_desalination_retaliation Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

text:
DESALINATION

Overnight, BlueGold impounds every rain gauge on the coast, plus one birdbath deemed a gateway instrument. Fines flutter on doors like municipal moths: jam jars, barrels, a child's paddling pool cited as illegal reservoir infrastructure. The tariff on the seawall has grown an appendix, bolted on in brass, covering fog. The widow stands in her doorway holding her citation and asks the street, in general, whether the sky has signed anything.

choices.text: Follow the fines upstream to the billing office

success.text: The billing office sits two days inland, in a building with no plumbing, which explains a great deal. There you meet Gerald from compliance, eleven years billing a sea he has never seen, a postcard of an entirely different ocean pinned above his desk for reference. He refunds your gauge fine on the spot, apologizing for a decimal, and you leave with four coins and the unsettling knowledge that the machine is staffed by nephews and postcards. The crew back home is less charmed that you shook hands with the enemy's stationery.
effects: schedule 4 r18_desalination_coalition Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

fail.text: Inland, the billing office mistakes your questions for an audit and treats you accordingly: coffee, terror, receipts. You are escorted floor to floor by trembling supervisors and handed eight coins in unmarked reimbursement before anyone checks your credentials. You take it. Word travels home ahead of you, and the bucket crew hears mostly the part where BlueGold paid you to leave smiling.
effects: schedule 4 r18_desalination_coalition Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Hide the neighborhood's jars before the patrol

outcomes.text: You move forty jam jars, two barrels, and the criminal birdbath into the chapel crypt by dawn, labeled as relics. The patrol will not search a crypt; there is a form for it, and nobody has ever survived the form. The widow's dew collection sleeps beside actual saints, which she considers an upgrade for the saints.
effects: schedule 4 r18_desalination_coalition Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

outcomes.text: You are halfway over a fence with a birdbath when the patrol rounds the corner, and there is no dignified end to that sentence. The bath survives; your elbow files a complaint. The patrol writes you up for transporting precipitation infrastructure at night, but three households watch you take the ticket for their jars, and that ledger keeps its own accounts.
effects: schedule 4 r18_desalination_coalition Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Demand the gauges back as impounded property

success.text: You argue that an impounded gauge goes on measuring, meaning BlueGold now operates forty unlicensed collection devices in its own evidence room. The hearing officer goes very still, the way men do when an org chart shifts beneath them. The gauges come home by cart within the week, each bearing a certificate of decommissioned menace, and the appendix on fog is quietly unbolted for review.
effects: schedule 4 r18_desalination_coalition Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

fail.text: The hearing lasts four minutes, three of them oath. Your claim is denied on the grounds that the gauges volunteered, a legal doctrine the officer invents while maintaining eye contact. The fines stand, the appendix on fog gains a subsection about your tone, and the council adds your name to the minutes under tried, at least.
effects: schedule 4 r18_desalination_coalition Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

text:
DESALINATION

Word of the crypt full of jam jars travels upstream, and by Sunday the coast has visitors: dry farmers, the laundry women, the ferry crew, all carrying their own BlueGold correspondence. Someone proposes a shared cistern behind the chapel, fed by every gutter that still believes rain is possible. The tariff on the seawall now has two appendices and a small roof to protect its lamination, which is more shelter than the birdbath ever got. The meter reader, off duty again, keeps standing near the cistern plans in a way no one is required to report.

choices.text: Put three coins into the cistern kitty

outcomes.text: Your three coins land in the pot beside pledges of tile, timber, and one wildly optimistic promise of pipe. The cistern rises in a weekend, guttered from eleven roofs, and the crypt jars are promoted to a proper reservoir with a lid and a ladle rota. BlueGold's patrol inspects it, finds no clause covering water that never touched their meters, and leaves visibly bereaved.
effects: schedule 5 r18_desalination_crisis Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

outcomes.text: You spend the build day on the cistern roof and part of it under a falling tile. The laundry women bind your head with professional disapproval of your technique, then feed you. The cistern gets built around your supervision, and the accident book gains its second entry under acts of enthusiasm, same handwriting.
effects: schedule 5 r18_desalination_crisis Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Ask the meter reader what he actually wants

outcomes.text: He wants, it turns out, to someday read Meter One, which is what BlueGold's paperwork calls the ocean. Nobody has read it in years; the figures are estimated inland by a compliance officer working from a postcard. You suggest the council commission its own tide readings, an honest meter at last, and he agrees so fast his lanyard spins. BlueGold has lost its only field instrument to the simple technique of asking him a question.
effects: schedule 5 r18_desalination_crisis Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

outcomes.text: You ask him in front of his supervisor, the one place the question cannot survive. He recites the mission statement about hydration solutions with his eyes fixed on the sea he is paid to estimate, and the supervisor logs the interaction as attempted tampering with company property, meaning him. The council spends a week smoothing it over, and the meter reader is reassigned to inland puddles, pending loyalty.
effects: schedule 5 r18_desalination_crisis Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Quietly sell cistern water to thirsty travelers

outcomes.text: The road trade is brisk: clean water, no meter, no questions, fourteen coins by Friday. Strictly speaking the ladle rota never authorized retail, and strictly speaking is now the only way anyone at the cistern speaks about you. A BlueGold circular cites the incident as proof the coast cannot govern water, and the patrol doubles, on the grounds that commerce needs protecting even from itself.

text:
DESALINATION

Barometers drop all along the coast on the morning the drought decides to end. A storm the size of an accounting error is coming in off the privatized ocean, and BlueGold responds by invoicing it in advance: every household receives a projected rainfall bill, itemized by roof area, payable before the first drop. The tariff on the seawall is amended to its maximum schedule, and the little roof over its lamination is reinforced, twice. The widow reads her pre-bill, looks at the sky, and announces that the sky can speak to her lawyer.

choices.text: Seek an injunction against billing the future

success.text: The magistrate reads BlueGold's projected rainfall invoice with the expression of a man handed a horoscope stamped overdue. He rules that weather cannot be billed before it occurs, occurs being, he notes, the operative word in most of reality. The pre-bills are suspended, and the council pins his ruling beside the rota, where documents go to be liked.
effects: schedule 2 r18_desalination_compromise Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

fail.text: BlueGold's advocate argues that forecasting is a service and the storm is therefore a deliverable. The magistrate, whose pension is administered by a BlueGold subsidiary, finds this closer to sane than you can afford. The pre-bills stand, now stamped judicially reviewed, which is somehow worse, like a nightmare with a certificate.
effects: schedule 2 r18_desalination_compromise Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Corner the market on barrels and tarps

outcomes.text: You buy every barrel, tarp, and spare bucket on the coast at drought prices and resell them at storm prices, a term you invent that afternoon. Fourteen coins, no waiting, and a queue that will not meet your eye. The cistern kitty asks if you would care to donate a barrel, and you experience the novel sensation of being fundraised at, politely, by a widow.
effects: schedule 2 r18_desalination_compromise Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Rig every roof and gutter to catch the storm

outcomes.text: The whole coast goes up ladders at once: gutters aimed, barrels chained, the cistern's lid propped open like a mouth at a banquet. The meter reader calculates catchment angles with terrifying joy, off the clock, applying company arithmetic to the common gutter. By nightfall the coast is one enormous instrument pointed at the sky, and for the first time in three years the weather feels like something arriving rather than something owed.
effects: schedule 2 r18_desalination_compromise Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

outcomes.text: You are on the chapel roof when the first gust arrives ahead of schedule and files you under descending moisture. The drop is short, the compost heap is forgiving, and the accident book's third entry reads acts of enthusiasm, airborne. The gutters get rigged around your recovery, and the crew now rates roofs by how far you would bounce.
effects: schedule 2 r18_desalination_compromise Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

text:
DESALINATION

Noon before landfall, BlueGold requests a meeting on neutral ground, which they propose to be their boardroom. The council counters with the fish market and wins, because the storm is not on BlueGold's side of the ledger either. The regional director of rain arrives with a settlement offer and the tariff's original signing pen, carried ceremonially, as though it were a sword. The offer: pumps switched on at last, in exchange for the council recognizing Schedule R as, quote, conceptually valid.

choices.text: Skip the table and switch the pumps on yourself

success.text: The pump house guard is inland staff, storm shy, and currently guarding it from a tavern. You throw the lever your grandmother's generation paid for, and the desalination pumps cough, shudder, and begin producing water like it never stopped being their job. The coast cheers; the council does not, having been mid negotiation when their leverage started audibly working without them. You are toasted in every kitchen and formally deplored in the minutes, which is a career.
effects: schedule 3 r18_desalination_reversal Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

fail.text: The pump house guard turns out to be the one BlueGold employee who believes in something, and what he believes in is the door. You come away with three cracked ribs and a citation for attempted operation of infrastructure. By evening the pump house has a second guard, a fence, and a flag, and the council spends its whole meeting agreeing not to say told you so aloud.
effects: schedule 3 r18_desalination_reversal Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Refuse any deal that splits the coast

outcomes.text: The director tries the classic: pumps for the fishing quarter first, uphill households later, pending metrics. The council answers with one voice, all taps or no signature, and the laundry women fold their arms in a way that has ended stronger men. The offer improves twice in an hour, which tells everyone what the first offer was.
effects: schedule 3 r18_desalination_reversal Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

effects: schedule 3 r18_desalination_reversal Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

outcomes.text: Holding one line for six hours in a fish market during a barometric crash turns out to be physical work. You faint briefly into the herring display, are revived with vinegar, and miss the exact moment the director concedes the uphill households. The deal is better because everyone stayed; the smell also stays, with you, for days.
effects: schedule 3 r18_desalination_reversal Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Accept, contingent on Schedule R's own fine print

outcomes.text: You agree that Schedule R is conceptually valid, provided every clause applies as written, to everyone, including its author. The director signs, delighted, sensing no trap because he has never read it either. Clause eleven, you note aloud once the ink dries, obliges the party of record to accept liability for all unauthorized deliveries onto its property, and the storm is due on BlueGold's ocean by midnight. The pen goes back into its velvet box a little too quickly.
effects: schedule 3 r18_desalination_reversal Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

outcomes.text: You spring clause eleven before the ink is dry, and the director simply initials an exemption in the margin, because it is his tariff and his margin. The council watches your trap get amended out of existence in real time. The pumps stay off pending goodwill, a substance now visibly rationed, and the minutes record that clever is not the same as signed.

text:
DESALINATION

Midnight, landfall. The storm comes ashore without checking in at reception and delivers, by BlueGold's own arithmetic, forty years of unauthorized rain in nine hours, most of it onto their privatized ocean. Every gutter, barrel, and cistern on the coast fills; the pre-bills dissolve on doorsteps into their component fibers. Under its little roof the tariff's lamination finally fails at one corner, and Schedule R begins, physically, to absorb the weather it was written to prevent. Inland, a compliance officer climbs into a cart, because if the sea intends to breach his spreadsheet he intends to meet it.

choices.text: Haul neighbors and barrels above the surge line

success.text: You spend the night in a chain of hands moving children, goats, and full barrels uphill, in that order of cooperation. The cistern holds, the crypt does a second tour of duty, and nobody is lost, which the accident book records as an act of everyone. Near dawn you find Gerald from compliance on the seawall, drenched, staring at his first ocean, holding the peeling tariff like a man checking a portrait against the sitter. He asks how anyone was ever supposed to meter this, and the answer arrives as more rain.
effects: schedule 4 r18_desalination_legacy Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

fail.text: You take the surge chest high steering a barrel raft of jam jars, and the sea, newly public spirited, tries to keep you. The ferry crew fishes you out by the collar with professional boredom. The jars mostly survive; your boots are now BlueGold's problem, somewhere under their ocean, unbilled.
effects: schedule 4 r18_desalination_legacy Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Serve BlueGold the storm's invoice under clause eleven

outcomes.text: By lamplight the council drafts the coast's first outgoing invoice: one storm, delivered unauthorized to the property of record, per Schedule R, rates as published. The total, at BlueGold's own maximum schedule, exceeds the company's founding capital, and their lawyers' first response is a request to interview the tariff's author, who has retired somewhere without weather. Payment is negotiated down to a simpler figure: the pumps, running, in perpetuity, and a mutual undertaking never to mention clause eleven again while keeping certified copies.
effects: schedule 4 r18_desalination_legacy Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

outcomes.text: Your invoice is returned marked insufficient postage, which is bold from a company whose ocean is currently in your living rooms. Their lawyers argue the storm was an act of god, and you point out that BlueGold's own tariff redefined god as an unregistered supplier two appendices ago. The hearing is scheduled for a date that does not exist in this calendar year, and the council files that under progress, barely.
effects: schedule 4 r18_desalination_legacy Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

choices.text: Salvage the flooded billing office's petty cash

outcomes.text: The billing annex on the quay floods to the windowsills, and you wade in among drifting invoices like jellyfish with totals. The petty cash box surrenders fourteen coins and a smell you will carry to your grave. On the way out you pass the ledger of everyone's fines, dissolving, and leave it to its bath; mercy and laziness, conveniently, point the same way tonight.
effects: schedule 4 r18_desalination_legacy Somewhere inland, your rain is still being billed

text:
DESALINATION

A month after the storm, the desalination pumps run day and night under council management, sounding like the coast clearing its throat. Water is metered now by a rule the widow wrote on one page: everyone's share first, surplus voted on, no lamination. In the council hall hangs Schedule R itself, retired, rippled with water damage, framed under glass above a small brass plate reading FOUNDING DOCUMENT, BY WHOSE FAILURE THIS HALL WAS PLUMBED. The meter reader takes tide readings for the public record, and Meter One, he reports, is doing fine.

choices.text: Draft the water charter from the tariff's own rate table

outcomes.text: You invert Schedule R's rate table so that every rate becomes a share, every penalty a duty, every appendix a holiday. The charter runs one page, unlaminated on principle, and is drafted so plainly that BlueGold's remaining lawyers can find nothing in it to bill. Visiting councils copy it out longhand, which the widow permits, on condition they also copy the accident book, for honesty.

You invert Schedule R's rate table so that every rate becomes a share, every penalty a duty, every appendix a holiday. The charter runs one page, unlaminated on principle, and is drafted so plainly that BlueGold's remaining lawyers can find nothing in it to bill. Visiting councils copy it out longhand, which the widow permits, on condition they also copy the accident book, for honesty.

Your inverted charter is elegant right up until the clause about fog, which you leave in as a joke. A literalist on the council reads it aloud, and the coast spends a full week debating dew rights before the widow gavels the entire topic into the sea. The charter passes, minus your favorite joke, and the minutes list you as author of the fog crisis, second, minor.

choices.text: Consult on BlueGold's rebrand as a wellness company

outcomes.text: BlueGold survives, restructured, and pays fourteen coins for your insight into coastal sentiment, which you deliver in the form of adjectives. The rebrand launches: the same pumps they no longer control, a new logo, a mission statement about hydration journeys. Your name appears in the brochure under community voices, and at the cistern your ladle privileges become, in the widow's word, pending.

choices.text: Take the seawall watch and train the next crew

success.text: You take the dawn watch on the seawall, training recruits in the rota, the pumps, and the approved technique for falling off roofs, on which you are the leading authority. Gerald keeps the public ledger now, relocated at his own request, a jam jar of storm water on his desk labeled paid in full. The council enters your name in the book it keeps for people it intends to keep, and the tide comes in on schedule, free of charge.

fail.text: Your first act as watch trainer is demonstrating the seawall's slipperiest stone, personally, at speed. The recruits fish you out, and the accident book gains its final entry under your name: acts of enthusiasm, emeritus. You are given the watch anyway, on the theory that nobody guards a coast better than someone the coast has repeatedly tried to keep.

text:
HAUNTED WAREHOUSE

Rain chases you into a warehouse on the quay, where the night shift is dead and still working. Afterlife Logistics never fired anyone; it reclassified death as extended unpaid leave, and the ghosts have been loading crates for three hundred years while payroll waits for them to produce a pulse. A punch-clock ghost drifts over and asks, with terrible hope, whether you are alive, and if so, whether you could sign something.

choices.text: Demand Afterlife Logistics answer for three centuries of ghost wages

outcomes.text: You file the grievance as next of kin to no one in particular. Afterlife Logistics responds in public, on letterhead, and the letterhead admits more than the letter does: there is a wage account for the dead, and it has been earning interest for somebody. The ghosts hold the paper like a lantern.
effects: schedule 3 r18_haunted_warehouse_assembly the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

outcomes.text: The hearing rules that the dead lack standing, which the dead find rich, since standing is one of the few things a warehouse job still lets them do. The punch-clock ghost is commended for his attendance. The grievance survives as a rumor with excellent posture.
effects: schedule 3 r18_haunted_warehouse_assembly the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

choices.text: Quietly invoice Afterlife Logistics for your discretion

outcomes.text: Afterlife Logistics pays you the way a landlord fixes a hinge, instantly and only because the alternative involves witnesses. The punch-clock ghost watches the coins change hands and marks something in his ledger. The dead take a long, professional note of what the living cost.
effects: schedule 3 r18_haunted_warehouse_assembly the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

choices.text: Sign the ghost's form and stay to see where it goes

outcomes.text: You sign. The pen is freezing and the ghosts treat your signature like a rare import. By morning the night shift has its first living member, which under the bylaws of Afterlife Logistics is either a scandal or a merger. The back pay of the dead becomes, for the first time in centuries, a live question.

text:
HAUNTED WAREHOUSE

The warehouse dead convene their first full meeting since the lighthouse fire, which some members still consider recent. Quorum is a problem: Afterlife Logistics wrote the bylaws, and they require a show of hands from members who can no longer reliably produce hands. The punch-clock ghost keeps the minutes anyway. He has kept the minutes for three hundred years. Nobody has ever asked to read them.

choices.text: Sell the ghosts meeting supplies at a haunting markup

outcomes.text: You supply candles, chalk, and a bell at prices that would embarrass a museum gift shop. The dead pay in salvage and do not haggle, which is somehow worse than haggling. The meeting proceeds. Your profit sits in your pocket with the specific weight of a thing the minutes will remember.
effects: schedule 4 r18_haunted_warehouse_retaliation the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

choices.text: Lend the ghosts your hands for the vote

outcomes.text: You raise your hand once per proxy, slowly, forty one times, while the punch-clock ghost confirms each vote against his ledger. It is the most bureaucratic seance in maritime history and it works. The dead pass their first motion in centuries: they are owed, and they intend to collect.

The ghosts thank you anyway, with the courtesy of people who have watched every kind of failure and rank yours as sincere.

choices.text: Point out the bylaws never say the hands must be attached

success.text: You read the clause aloud twice, once for meaning and once for effect. The bylaws, drafted to exclude the dead, contain no anatomy requirements whatsoever. Dock gloves are procured and floated. Afterlife Logistics receives the motion, carried unanimously by empty gloves, and files it, because filing is the one reflex it has left.

fail.text: The chair rules your glove maneuver clever, which in meeting procedure is a way of saying dead on arrival. The motion fails. Still, the gallery got the joke, and a laughing ghost is a ghost remembering it used to be a person. Several of them see you out like old friends.

text:
HAUNTED WAREHOUSE

Afterlife Logistics responds to the grievance by hiring a workforce decommissioning consultant, which is the coastal way of saying exorcist. He arrives with a bell, a candle, an invoice, and the confidence of a man who has never met a workforce with nothing left to lose, medically. The contract calls the loading bays a nonperforming asset. The ghosts call them home, although never where the consultant can hear, because home is the sort of word that ends up in a report.

choices.text: Stand between the exorcist and the night shift

outcomes.text: You plant yourself in the bay doors and explain that the consultant is welcome to decommission you first. He checks his scope of work. Living obstruction is not in it, and doing anything not in a scope of work frightens consultants more than the dead ever could. The night shift loads on behind you, quietly, like a tide refusing to go out.
effects: schedule 5 r18_haunted_warehouse_coalition the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

outcomes.text: The consultant works around you, the way water works around a stone, and two of the older ghosts are rung out of the west bay before the candle gutters. The night shift gathers what is left of their rota and stands very still. You learn that grief, among the dead, looks like scheduling.
effects: schedule 5 r18_haunted_warehouse_coalition the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

choices.text: File an injunction: the bays are an active workplace, not an asset

outcomes.text: The clerk of the debt court, to everyone's surprise including hers, accepts the filing. An active workplace cannot be decommissioned while a dispute is pending, and the ghosts have helpfully been pending since before the court existed. The exorcist is sent home unpaid, which he describes as the most frightening thing he has ever seen.
effects: schedule 5 r18_haunted_warehouse_coalition the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

outcomes.text: The court finds that the dead cannot be workers because workers must be insurable, and the dead are, in the underwriter's words, one hundred percent preexisting condition. The exorcist's invoice is approved as a business expense. The punch-clock ghost copies the ruling into his ledger, which is beginning to read like scripture.
effects: schedule 5 r18_haunted_warehouse_coalition the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

choices.text: Ring the consultant's own bell before he can

success.text: You ring his bell, blow out his candle, and hand him his invoice marked received. Ritually speaking this concludes his engagement, a technicality he discovers in front of everyone. Exorcism, like most consulting, turns out to be mostly billing, and you have just closed the account. The night shift gives you the closest thing the dead have to applause: total, attentive silence.
effects: schedule 5 r18_haunted_warehouse_coalition the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

fail.text: You reach for the bell and find that exorcists insure their equipment against exactly you. The recoil puts you in the harbor. The consultant fishes you out, cites a duty of care, and adds the rescue to his invoice. Above you, arranged along the crane, the entire night shift pointedly fails to laugh.
effects: schedule 5 r18_haunted_warehouse_coalition the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

text:
HAUNTED WAREHOUSE

The living dockworkers finally send a delegation, and it is not a friendly one. Free ghost labor has been holding their wages down for generations: why hire a stevedore when the dead load for nothing and never file for leave? The two shifts meet at the bay doors at dusk, the hour they share, and discover they have the same employer, the same grievance, and the same punch clock. Nobody wants to be the first to say it out loud.

choices.text: Ask both shifts what Afterlife Logistics pays the other one

outcomes.text: The answer lands like cargo dropped from a great height. The living believed the dead were paid in rest. The dead believed the living were paid in wages. Both have been loading against a rival that does not exist. It is the fastest friendship ever formed on this coast, and it begins, as most do, with embarrassment.
effects: schedule 2 r18_haunted_warehouse_crisis the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

outcomes.text: The question starts an argument about who suffered more, which the dead win on tenure and the living win on volume. The delegations separate to sulk. Still, the question is now down in the minutes, and the minutes have a way of outliving a sulk.
effects: schedule 2 r18_haunted_warehouse_crisis the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

choices.text: Offer to broker between the shifts, for a finder's fee

outcomes.text: Both sides pay you to carry messages across a doorway they could shout through. It is excellent money for honest work, minus the honest part. The shifts do eventually talk, around you rather than through you, and your fee enters the punch-clock ghost's ledger under a heading you are not shown.

choices.text: Hold the doors at dusk and make the two shifts talk

success.text: The bay doors stay open through the dusk and the two shifts file in together, the living stamping rain off their boots, the dead bringing the cold in politely. One grievance is drafted: wages for the living, back pay for the dead, and the clock to answer for both. Afterlife Logistics now faces the one thing its actuaries never priced: a workforce that includes its own casualties.
effects: schedule 2 r18_haunted_warehouse_crisis the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

fail.text: The joint meeting collapses over seniority, since the dead technically have it and the living technically need it. But the two rotas end up pinned to the same board, touching at one corner, and on this coast that counts as a treaty.
effects: schedule 2 r18_haunted_warehouse_crisis the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

text:
HAUNTED WAREHOUSE

Afterlife Logistics posts a notice: the loading bays are scheduled for asset retirement on Thursday, effective sunrise. Demolition, in the company's grammar. The bays are the ghosts' anchor; when the roof goes, the night shift goes with it, unpaid, into whatever waits after the afterlife, which not even the punch-clock ghost has seen the paperwork for. The notice thanks everyone for three hundred years of service. It is the first time the company has acknowledged the service at all.

choices.text: Buy salvage rights to the bays before Thursday

outcomes.text: You purchase the demolition salvage for a sum that makes the clerk check the ledger twice. Legally you now own the timbers, the cranes, and the doors: everything a ghost holds onto. It is a hostage position, and everyone knows it, and the dead regard you with the wary respect owed to a person whose help is indistinguishable from leverage.
effects: schedule 3 r18_haunted_warehouse_compromise the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

choices.text: Refuse to leave: both shifts, all hours, until Thursday blinks

success.text: The living take the doors and the dead take everything else. The demolition crew arrives at sunrise to find the site staffed beyond capacity by parties they cannot legally disperse or plausibly frighten. Work continues in shifts, in defiance, in daylight. The notice comes down by noon, pinned under a single word in the punch-clock ghost's copperplate: DENIED.

fail.text: The crew breaches the west bay before the occupation holds, and one wall comes down with three centuries of chalk tallies still on it. The ghosts stand in the dust reading the numbers while they fade. The east bay holds. What is left of the night shift will not move again, and now neither will you.

choices.text: Serve a heritage claim: the bays are a gravesite in active use

outcomes.text: The registrar concedes that a building continuously occupied by its own dead is, at minimum, a cemetery, and cemeteries cannot be retired for portfolio reasons without an act nobody wants their name on. Demolition halts. Afterlife Logistics discovers it has spent three hundred years operating a graveyard without a license, and the fines are magnificent.

outcomes.text: The registrar rules the ghosts are not remains but residue, a distinction he does not survive with dignity, because the night shift attends the hearing in person and stands quietly at the back being extremely remains. The claim fails. The story of the hearing does not. By morning the whole coast knows what Thursday means.

text:
HAUNTED WAREHOUSE

Afterlife Logistics offers terms. In full and final settlement of centuries of unpaid spectral labor: one memorial plaque, bay-adjacent, plus a commemorative candle allowance, renewable annually at the company's discretion. The plaque text is included for approval. It says THEY WERE PART OF THE FAMILY. The punch-clock ghost reads it twice and asks, in his careful clerk's voice, whether the family got paid.

choices.text: Reject it together and publish the offer on every dock

outcomes.text: The night shift says no, the day shift says no, and the offer is nailed to the harbor board between the tide tables and the fish prices, where everyone can read exactly what three hundred years is worth to Afterlife Logistics. Shame is not a wage, but on this coast it compounds faster.
effects: schedule 4 r18_haunted_warehouse_reversal the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

outcomes.text: The vote is no, but publishing costs you: the company recalls a barge contract and two of the loudest dockers lose their berths within the week. The plaque, refused, goes up anyway near the office door, where the rain gets at the word FAMILY early and often.
effects: schedule 4 r18_haunted_warehouse_reversal the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

choices.text: Accept the candle allowance, then read the fine print aloud

success.text: The allowance, you announce to the assembled shifts, is denominated in candles per grievance per annum, and does not cap the number of grievances. The dead begin filing at once: one candle each for every unpaid shift since the lighthouse fire. By Friday the company owes more candlelight than the coast has wax, and accounting is begging to reopen wage talks in any currency but flame.
effects: schedule 4 r18_haunted_warehouse_reversal the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

fail.text: Legal notices a currency clause faster than you can exploit it and the allowance is restated in company scrip, redeemable at the company store, which burned down in the lighthouse fire. The dead appreciate the attempt. The dead are, on the subject of company arithmetic, incapable of surprise.
effects: schedule 4 r18_haunted_warehouse_reversal the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

choices.text: Ask the punch-clock ghost what settlement he would accept

success.text: The question stops the room, because in three hundred years of minutes nobody has asked him anything. He consults his own ledger for a long moment. What he wants, he says, is an audit. Every hour, every name, every bay, read into the record. Not the money: the acknowledgment that the money is owed. You realize you have just heard the real demand, from the only one who has been counting.
effects: schedule 4 r18_haunted_warehouse_reversal the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

fail.text: He declines to answer while on shift, which he always is. That is the answer, and everyone in the bay understands it except the company, which is the point. The offer expires unaccepted. The clock ticks on, patient as compound interest.
effects: schedule 4 r18_haunted_warehouse_reversal the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

text:
HAUNTED WAREHOUSE

During the audit the punch-clock ghost asked for, the time clock itself is opened for the first time since installation, and the mechanism inside is not a mechanism. It is ledgers, wound tight around the spindle, three hundred years of them in the punch-clock ghost's copperplate: every shift, every name, every unpaid hour, priced in the wages of its own decade. The total, compounded, exceeds the value of Afterlife Logistics. The clock was never keeping time. It was keeping accounts. And one page, near the spindle, records the wages the company did pay: to the ghost foreman who reported every troublemaker for two centuries, in candle-smoke scrip, one shift of rest at a time.

choices.text: Enter the whole ledger into the record, foreman's page included

outcomes.text: The audit takes nine days and the clerk of the debt court runs out of ink twice. When it is done, the debt of Afterlife Logistics to its own dead is a matter of public record, and the foreman's page sits in the file where posterity can weigh it. The dead, it turns out, wanted the record more than the revenge. The record is permanent. Revenge was only ever a shift.
effects: schedule 5 r18_haunted_warehouse_legacy the warehouse ghosts' association will need you again

The debt stands anyway, vast and undeniable. The foreman takes the sealed page hardest of all: being protected by the company is the only sentence he was afraid of.

choices.text: Quietly sell the total to the company's creditors

You are paid in full before anyone else. The dead note, without heat, that you have made the rich poorer and no ghost richer, and the punch-clock ghost opens a fresh page with your name at the top.

choices.text: Let the night shift decide what happens to the foreman

outcomes.text: The vote is held at dusk with both shifts present, and the sentence is worse than exile and better than mercy: he keeps his rest, and he reads the ledger aloud, every unpaid name, one shift at a time, until the roll is complete. It will take him years. He accepts with something that has been trying to become relief for two centuries. The dead do not forgive quickly. They schedule it.

text:
HAUNTED WAREHOUSE

The settlement conference convenes on neutral ground, which on this coast means the tavern. Afterlife Logistics is insolvent in every direction, and back pay for the dead has become the estate's senior debt, ahead of the banks, who have opinions. The only unresolved question is the one nobody thought to draft: what currency the dead will accept. The night shift confers. The punch-clock ghost rises with the night shift's answer, holding the clock's spindle like a gavel: the dead demand their time back. Paid leave, in arrears. They would like, at last, to clock out.

choices.text: Buy the warehouse cheap out of the insolvency

outcomes.text: You acquire the bays for scrap value plus the assumption of one unusual covenant: the ghosts' leave must be honored by every future owner, forever, senior to any mortgage. You are now a landlord whose tenants are owed three hundred years of rest and cannot be evicted by anything less than sunrise itself. The banks call the title poisoned. The dead call you, with unnerving warmth, boss.

You acquire the bays for scrap value plus the assumption of one unusual covenant: the ghosts' leave must be honored by every future owner, forever, senior to any mortgage. You are now a landlord whose tenants are owed three hundred years of rest and cannot be evicted by anything less than sunrise itself. The banks call the title poisoned. The dead call you, with unnerving warmth, boss.

choices.text: Stand witness as the first shift clocks out

outcomes.text: It happens at dusk, in reverse order of seniority, so the newest dead go first and the punch-clock ghost goes last of all. Each one rings the clock once and is, in the fullest sense of the term, off. The clock's final entry is in a hand that shakes for the first time in three centuries: PAID IN FULL. The bays fall quiet. The quiet is not empty; it is finished. The coast will argue for a generation about what was owed, and every argument will begin with the audit you helped win.

It happens at dusk, in reverse order of seniority, so the newest dead go first and the punch-clock ghost goes last of all. Each one rings the clock once and is, in the fullest sense of the term, off. The clock's final entry is in a hand that shakes for the first time in three centuries: PAID IN FULL. The bays fall quiet. The quiet is not empty; it is finished. The coast will argue for a generation about what was owed, and every argument will begin with the audit you helped win.

Not everyone goes. A dozen of the older ghosts refuse the settlement, on the grounds that they have watched every reform on this coast get repealed and intend to stay on as inspectors. The clock-out proceeds without them. They remain in the rafters to this day, retired in every respect except vigilance, the coast's first and only volunteer wage enforcement agency.

choices.text: Propose the clock be rehung as the harbor's bell

success.text: The motion carries every vote in the room, living and dead and one abstention from the banks out of spite. The clock is rehung over the shared hall, and from that day it rings shift changes instead of counting them: an instrument of surveillance retrained, in retirement, as an instrument of rest. Dockers set their lives by it. Very few of them know it still keeps the ledgers inside, in case.

fail.text: The banks claim the clock as an asset, appraise it, open it, and find three hundred years of wage ledgers where the movement should be, at which point they appraise it again at nothing and abandon the claim in writing. The dead accept the clock back with thanks. Some assets, the punch-clock ghost notes in the minutes, are only worthless to the people who owe what is written in them.

text:
HEALER STRIKE

At the doors of MercyCare Revenue Temple, a dying man is counting out his admission fee while a chorister in billing vestments chants the intake codes over him. Inside, the healers have stopped being polite about it: they have written a grievance on the back of a discharge form, and the surgeon who once billed the Temple for the concept of Tuesday has signed it twice, once as witness and once as attending physician to the whole situation. They are looking for one more signature.

choices.text: Sign it and chip into the door fund

outcomes.text: You sign under the surgeon, which she notes is traditionally where the anesthetist goes. Your three coins go into a biscuit tin marked DOOR FEES, PAID BADLY, ON PRINCIPLE, which will now pay admission for the dying from the outside in. The night kitchen, hearing of the tin, pledges soup to anyone the Temple turns away. The chorister keeps chanting intake codes at the tin, but the tin has no account, and a thing with no account cannot be denied.
effects: schedule 5 r18_healer_strike_assembly Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

outcomes.text: You shoulder the dying man past the intake lectern, and a verger of collections blesses you across the ribs with the donation paddle. The man gets a bed, you get a bruise shaped like generosity, and the street outside develops a low administrative growl. The surgeon signs you into the rota anyway, coding the bruise as a joining fee, waived.
effects: schedule 5 r18_healer_strike_assembly Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

choices.text: File the grievance through the Office of Miracles Denied

outcomes.text: The Office of Miracles Denied has never met a document it could not refuse, but the grievance is written on their own discharge form, which they are canonically required to process. A clerk stamps it RECEIVED with the reverence of a man baptizing a bomb. Somewhere upstairs, the fee schedule flinches. The surgeon attaches her Tuesday invoice as supporting scripture.
effects: schedule 5 r18_healer_strike_assembly Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

outcomes.text: Your grievance comes back within the hour, denied under code MC-12, insufficient faith in the appeals process. The surgeon reads the denial aloud in her best liturgical voice and files it under evidence, a growing genre. The Temple, feeling vindicated, raises the admission fee to cover the cost of denying you.

The pay is fourteen coins and the work is reading the fee schedule aloud to people too weak to argue, which the Temple calls pastoral care. The healers watch you take the sash. The surgeon sends you an invoice for the concept of loyalty, terms net thirty, interest accruing.

text:
HEALER STRIKE

Midnight in the linen cloister, and the healers hold their meeting between shelves of folded shrouds, because it is the one room the Temple never audits. The night matron takes attendance by exhaustion, most severe first. On the agenda: the Temple has introduced a convenience fee for dying during business hours, and the surgeon's invoice for Tuesday has entered its second cycle of appeal, which she considers a good sign, since it means they have not ruled out Tuesday entirely.

choices.text: Draft a counter-liturgy of billing codes

success.text: You and the surgeon spend two hours composing the Responses to Denial, a call and answer in perfect Temple format: for every code that refuses care, a code that performs it anyway and bills the refusal as the underlying condition. The matron reads it once and laughs for the first time in a fiscal quarter. Copies circulate by morning, disguised as remittance advice. Even the shrouds seem better folded.
effects: schedule 2 r18_healer_strike_retaliation Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

fail.text: Temple format is unforgiving, and your counter-liturgy has a rhythm problem: you rhyme a diagnosis code with a funeral code and accidentally invoice a living patient for his own burial. The Temple processes it instantly, the one fast thing it has ever done. The matron spends her night unwinding the claim while the surgeon takes notes on what the Temple approves when it is not paying attention.
effects: schedule 2 r18_healer_strike_retaliation Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

choices.text: Sell the meeting's minutes to the deacon of copays

outcomes.text: The deacon of copays receives you in his office, formerly a chapel, still excellent acoustics for guilt. He pays fourteen coins, files your minutes under Prophecy, and asks, softly, whether the healers ever mention him, and if so, fondly. You take the money and leave the question. Back in the cloister, the matron adds an agenda item: leaks, and the pricing thereof.
effects: schedule 2 r18_healer_strike_retaliation Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

outcomes.text: The rota is literally a laundry list, care disguised as linen deliveries: two fevers, one crushed hand, a birth if it comes to that. You take the Tuesday column, which the surgeon calls the disputed territory. By dawn the ward has treated eleven people the Temple never billed, a sensation the Temple experiences as a haunting.

outcomes.text: You take the overnight column and the overnight column takes most of you: by the fourth fever you are dosing yourself from the samples drawer and calling it staff appreciation. The matron sends you home before you become a patient, which would ruin the paperwork. Outside, word spreads that the healers treat people for free now, and the district begins, quietly, to expect it.
effects: schedule 2 r18_healer_strike_retaliation Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

text:
HEALER STRIKE

Three padlocks appeared on the medicine stores overnight, blessed and itemized, beside a notice that supplies now release only against a preauthorization hymn sung in the presence of a licensed cantor. The cantor is on leave. The surgeon's Tuesday claim has been denied pending proof of onset, meaning the Temple wants documentation of when Tuesday began. She has requested a calendar and a notary.

choices.text: Take the storeroom door off its blessed hinges

success.text: The hinges come away with a sound like a choir clearing its throat, and for one night the medicine moves the way medicine was always rumored to. Fever bark and clean thread go to anyone standing near a healer, which turns out to be the whole street. The matron disapproves formally while carrying boxes, having found the two positions compatible. The Temple guards will remember your face, and say so, in writing.
effects: schedule 3 r18_healer_strike_coalition Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

fail.text: The second padlock is load bearing in a spiritual sense, and the door wins the argument. You wake on a gurney with three fewer working ribs and a guard detail newly issued halberds, booked by the Temple as a safety investment. The surgeon splints you free of charge and bills the Temple for the door's aggression, code pending. The stores stay shut.
effects: schedule 3 r18_healer_strike_coalition Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

choices.text: Run the medicine out through the laundry chute

outcomes.text: Shroud bundles go down the chute with fever bark folded into the hems, and the night laundress, who owes the matron a favor from a difficult birth, asks no questions in a very pointed way. Care travels the building disguised as bedding. The Temple's inventory reports a miracle of shrinkage. The surgeon invoices them for storage of the miracle.
effects: schedule 3 r18_healer_strike_coalition Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

outcomes.text: The chute jams at the third floor and you climb in after the bundle, because of course you do. You emerge in the sorting room bruised, dusted in lint, holding the medicine like a caught baby, in front of two vergers doing a surprise count. They log an intruder in the linens; the district hears a healer got hurt smuggling fever bark, and takes it personally.
effects: schedule 3 r18_healer_strike_coalition Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

choices.text: Cite the founding charter: medicine belongs to the sick

outcomes.text: The founding charter hangs in the Temple lobby, framed, dusted weekly, read never. Clause two, in the founders' own hand: the stores are held in trust for the sick, without toll. A trust, the surgeon observes, is a debt with witnesses, and she has just made the entire lobby into witnesses. The padlocks stay on, but now they are evidence, and vergers begin unlocking things preemptively whenever she approaches.
effects: schedule 3 r18_healer_strike_coalition Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

outcomes.text: The Temple's advocate produces an amendment, two centuries old, defining the sick as parties current on their accounts. Everyone else is pre-sick, a state not covered. Your citation is thanked for its engagement with the tradition. The fee schedule gains a footnote, and the footnote gains a fee.
effects: schedule 3 r18_healer_strike_coalition Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

text:
HEALER STRIKE

Word of the free ward has traveled, and now the porters, the gravediggers, and the entire night kitchen crowd the linen cloister with a proposal: everybody in. The surgeon has a larger idea, drawn on a shroud in charcoal. Stop refusing to bill, she says: treat everyone free, then bill the Temple for it, itemized, in their own format, until the machine chokes on its native tongue. She has already drafted the first invoice, and it is for Tuesday, on behalf of everyone who survived one.

choices.text: Bring the gravediggers and porters into the rota

success.text: The gravediggers turn out to know the Temple's schedule intimately, being its outcomes department; the porters know every corridor the vergers avoid after dark. You spend the night matching trades to shifts like the matron of some larger, stranger hospital. By morning the rota covers four professions and both ends of life. The surgeon lists them all as billable providers at the Temple's own posted rates, which never imagined a gravedigger reading them.
effects: schedule 4 r18_healer_strike_crisis Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

fail.text: Trades have their own weather: the porters want the dawn shifts the night kitchen already claimed, and an old dispute over a borrowed handcart chooses tonight to reconvene. You spend the meeting as furniture between two shouting parties and absorb an elbow meant for a pastry cook. The rota survives, but the district hears the healers' friends yelling in a linen room and draws its own conclusions.
effects: schedule 4 r18_healer_strike_crisis Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

choices.text: Help the surgeon draft the first great invoice

outcomes.text: You learn the codes the way the Temple intends, as scripture, then deploy them the way scripture gets deployed, selectively and with total confidence. The invoice runs forty pages: every stitched wound, every cooled fever, every death made gentle, each item priced from the Temple's own missal, payable on receipt. The deacon of copays reads it twice and reports a feeling he cannot code. It is legally flawless, which is the cruelest thing anyone has ever done to them.
effects: schedule 4 r18_healer_strike_crisis Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

outcomes.text: Ambition outruns doctrine: you cite the missal's rate for resurrection, which turns out to be aspirational, a code reserved for services never yet rendered. The whole invoice is quarantined pending theological review. The surgeon is not angry, she is fascinated, and begins a second draft on a fresh shroud. The Temple crier announces that the healers tried to bill for a miracle, and some of the district believes him.
effects: schedule 4 r18_healer_strike_crisis Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

choices.text: Skim a service fee off the free ward's kitty

outcomes.text: Administration is a service, you tell the tin, and services have fees; the tin says nothing, being a tin. Fourteen coins travel from the shroud fund to your pocket with the smoothness of established practice. The matron recounts it twice, says nothing, and moves the tin somewhere only the exhausted know. Trust, unlike Tuesday, is not billable, and now you know why.
effects: schedule 4 r18_healer_strike_crisis Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

text:
HEALER STRIKE

The Grand Auditor of MercyCare has arrived in a palanquin of filing cabinets, come to rule on forty pages of free care billed back to the Temple. The audit convenes in the chapel of adjudication, where the fee schedule is displayed like a relic and the surgeon's Tuesday claim sits at the top of the docket, flagged in three colors, because nobody below the rank of auditor is authorized to decide whether a day of the week can be a diagnosis.

choices.text: Defend the invoices line by line before the Auditor

outcomes.text: Line forty takes an hour: dressing changes, at the missal's posted rate. The Auditor keeps waiting for the fraudulent item and keeps finding care, correctly coded, which is worse. By evening he has approved eleven pages out of professional reflex, stopping only when he sees where it leads. The Tuesday claim he defers upward, to a tribunal that does not yet exist, thereby creating one.
effects: schedule 5 r18_healer_strike_compromise Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

outcomes.text: The Auditor rules the invoices inadmissible on grounds of sequence: healing occurred before authorization, which reverses the sacred order of operations. Care, he explains gently, is what the Temple bills for, so care performed unbilled is a kind of counterfeiting. The chapel takes a moment to admire the shape of that. The surgeon requests his reasoning in writing, for her collection.
effects: schedule 5 r18_healer_strike_compromise Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

choices.text: Sell the Auditor your testimony, lightly edited

outcomes.text: You testify that the free ward exists, which is true, and that the surgeon leads it, which is public, and you shade nothing but your tone. The Auditor pays fourteen coins for the performance and marks you down as a cooperative element. The matron watches from the gallery with the expression she reserves for bedsores. That night the free ward changes its passwords, which are people.
effects: schedule 5 r18_healer_strike_compromise Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

choices.text: Have every healer claim the invoices as their own

success.text: The Auditor asks who authorized the billing, and the room stands one by one: the matron, the porters, a gravedigger holding his hat, the night kitchen still in flour. Each states for the record that the invoices are theirs. The Auditor cannot sanction the entire staff of a functioning hospital without becoming the emergency his audit exists to prevent, a fact he writes down slowly. The surgeon stands last and bills him for the room's time, itemized by rank.
effects: schedule 5 r18_healer_strike_compromise Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

fail.text: The plan requires everyone to stand at once, and the night kitchen, exhausted past choreography, stands early, alone, terrified. In the scramble to cover them you claim the invoice signatures as yours, which is at least a speaking role. You leave the chapel with a formal censure and a shoulder strained by the gallery's congratulations. The district hears that one person confessed to caring for everybody, and finds the Temple's arithmetic on this insulting.
effects: schedule 5 r18_healer_strike_compromise Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

text:
HEALER STRIKE

A settlement arrives by liveried courier: the Temple will forgive the free ward everything, restore the medicine stores, and drop all censures, provided the healers withdraw one item. Not the forty pages. Just Tuesday. The deacon of copays delivers the terms in person and lingers at the door, wanting someone to say he did his best. The surgeon reads the offer and smiles like a woman whose invoice has finally been taken seriously.

choices.text: Slip into the Temple archive and read their own books

success.text: The archive smells of incense and compound interest. Ledger after ledger shows the Temple billing itself: rent for the chapel, charged to the chapel; a tithe on the reliquary, paid late, by the reliquary, with penalties; a levy on the grain sheds that stops the moment your copies reach the granary clerk, who pays four coins for the set and a lifetime of indignation. The healers are grateful for the originals, and note, for the record, that you went in without telling anyone. The surgeon files the Temple's self-billing under prior art.
effects: schedule 2 r18_healer_strike_reversal Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

fail.text: Behind the settlement you find its shadow file, and in it the Temple's private price for Tuesday: a number so large it has its own guard rotation. You also find a drawer of unclaimed patient refunds, decades deep, and your pockets reach an executive decision. Eight coins of somebody's reimbursement later, you leave richer and lighter in ways that do not cancel. The matron notices the incense on your coat and does not ask, which is its own bill.
effects: schedule 2 r18_healer_strike_reversal Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

choices.text: Refuse the deal, keep Tuesday on the bill

outcomes.text: The healers vote by show of bandaged hands: Tuesday stays on the bill. The matron explains the rejection to the deacon the way she delivers prognoses, kindly and without exits: the free ward is not a bargaining position, it is a ward. The deacon writes REJECTED in beautiful calligraphy and, underneath, in pencil, good for you, then erases it, mostly. The surgeon donates that week's Tuesday to the tin.
effects: schedule 2 r18_healer_strike_reversal Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

outcomes.text: Tuesday stays, and the Temple's patience goes: intake vergers begin requiring the free ward's patients to prove they are not patients, a form with no correct answers. You spend a day as a human waiting room, absorbing the queue's despair and one flung inkwell. The district watches the Temple punish a hospital for hospitaling, and its opinion curdles into something with a schedule.
effects: schedule 2 r18_healer_strike_reversal Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

choices.text: Negotiate the settlement one billing code at a time

success.text: You accept the settlement in principle, then request the definitions. Two days of clause work later, forgive means pay, restore means expand, and withdraw means submit through alternate channels, all in the Temple's own defined-terms format, initialed by a deacon who badly wanted the meeting to end warmly. The Temple has signed its surrender in its native tongue. The surgeon frames the definitions page next to her Tuesday invoice, family at last.
effects: schedule 2 r18_healer_strike_reversal Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

fail.text: The Temple's advocate defines faster than you do, and by clause nine the free ward has become a pilot program, revocable at any fiscal solstice. You catch it before signing, barely, and walk out holding nothing but everyone's original positions and a headache in Temple format. The deacon looks genuinely sorry, twice, once per side.
effects: schedule 2 r18_healer_strike_reversal Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

text:
HEALER STRIKE

After dark, the deacon of copays comes to the linen cloister carrying the master code book and the face of a man mid-conversion. He has run the Tuesday claim through final adjudication, he says, and it cannot be denied: refusing it requires ruling Tuesday medically irrelevant, which voids every fee the Temple has ever charged on one, four centuries of Tuesdays, refundable with interest. Deny the surgeon and the Temple owes everyone. Pay her and it owes one woman. He is asking, please, to be caught on the right side.

choices.text: Pass the tin to fund the claim's final filing fee

outcomes.text: The final filing fee is three coins, priced as a joke by a Temple that never imagined anyone getting this far. The tin covers it with the whole cloister watching, each coin dropped like a vow. The deacon carries the filing out at a pace canon law would call devotional. The surgeon signs the claim and adds the tin as a dependent.
effects: schedule 3 r18_healer_strike_legacy Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

outcomes.text: The tin runs short, so you make up the difference in kind, donating tomorrow's meals and a little of tonight's blood to free up hands for the filing. The claim goes in at dawn. By noon, a rumor that the Temple might owe everybody money is moving through the district like weather, the kind that lifts roofs.
effects: schedule 3 r18_healer_strike_legacy Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

choices.text: Enter the code book itself into the tribunal record

outcomes.text: The master code book enters the record as exhibit one, and the tribunal must now read the machinery aloud: code upon code for refusing the dying, in liturgical order, each with its price. Read silently, the book governed; read aloud, it confesses. The tribunal's own scribe stops transcribing to listen. Above it all sits the Tuesday claim, looking less like a joke and more like the only honest page.
effects: schedule 3 r18_healer_strike_legacy Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

outcomes.text: The Temple's advocate objects that the code book is proprietary scripture, and the tribunal, tenderly, agrees: the machinery may govern the public without being shown to it. The deacon takes the book back like a man rebuffed at his own confession. What the room remembers, though, is the Temple insisting its holy text must never be read aloud, which is not the victory they seem to think.
effects: schedule 3 r18_healer_strike_legacy Tuesday remains under appeal at the Revenue Temple.

choices.text: Name your price for losing the code book overnight

outcomes.text: The Temple's advocate meets you behind the reliquary with fourteen coins and no eye contact, and by morning the master code book has been misfiled, an act of god according to the paperwork, though god was not billed for it. The deacon searches the cloister twice, then stops searching faces, which is somehow worse. The surgeon refiles the Tuesday claim from memory, since of course she knows it by heart. Some things can be lost for money; her arithmetic is not among them.
