outcomes.text: The registry rejects the filing on the grounds that the minutes lack a mission statement, a values matrix, and margins. The clerk offers to add margins; the registrar does not laugh, which is officially a finding. The Group's counsel notes the failed attempt and lodges a countermotion titled 'heritage risk,' which now hangs over the co-op like weather that bills by the hour.
effects: schedule 4 r18_coop_capture_legacy The night assembly saves you a seat on the settle

text:
COOP CAPTURE

Three weeks on, the bakery smells like itself, and the long bench by the oven answers to 'the settle' again, as it did for two hundred years before the ferns. The former consultant is here at four in the morning, unemployed by consolidation and unconvertible back, wearing an apron over the last of his good shirts. He has asked, formally, to learn bread from the beginning. The night assembly must now decide what a co-op does with a defector who knows exactly how bakeries get taken.

choices.text: Sponsor his membership and take him on your shift

success.text: You stand him before the assembly, and he offers the only references he has: seventeen bakeries, named, with what was done to each and how to see it coming. The clerk enters him in the ownership ledger under 'apprentice, reformed, watch his hands near paperwork,' and the vote carries on the grounds that the oven belongs to them as feeds it, and he has volunteered for the four o'clock feeding. He cries into the flour, which the minutes record as 'humidity.' You are named his keeper, which is a role, which means the rota knows your name now.

fail.text: Sponsorship requires a demonstration of trust, so you put him on the oven with you, and his first stroke with the peel sends a tray of tins at your shins with a sincerity no seminar ever taught him. The assembly, watching you hop, votes him in anyway; a co-op that survived the Group can survive his elbows. The street hears the crash and the cheering and adds both to the bakery's growing legend, which has begun drawing curious crowds at unhelpful hours.

choices.text: Set him to writing the Group's tricks into plain warnings

outcomes.text: You give him a desk, which he understands, and a task, which he becomes: translating the Group's playbook into a plain broadsheet titled HOW YOUR BAKERY GETS EATEN, first sign to last. The mill and the greengrocer post copies by their scales, and two bakeries downriver read theirs in time. The old mission statement, still laminated, finds work as the rain cover for the bread cart, protecting loaves, which is the first sentence of it that has ever been true.

You give him a desk, which he understands, and a task, which he becomes: translating the Group's playbook into a plain broadsheet titled HOW YOUR BAKERY GETS EATEN, first sign to last. The mill and the greengrocer post copies by their scales, and two bakeries downriver read theirs in time. The old mission statement, still laminated, finds work as the rain cover for the bread cart, protecting loaves, which is the first sentence of it that has ever been true.

The broadsheet is accurate, which is the problem; the Group's counsel calls it defamation with page numbers and serves the co-op a writ that outweighs a proving batch. The former consultant reads the writ, professionally, and pronounces it billable, survivable, and grounds for a second edition. The co-op now has a legal fund, a former enemy managing it, and, the clerk notes, better attendance than ever.

choices.text: Broker his old playbook to the highest outside bidder

outcomes.text: The Group's playbook has other admirers: a syndicate with firm opinions about who should own everything pays cash for a copy, no questions, no receipts. The former consultant watches you hand over the methods he defected from and says nothing, in his old fluent way. Somewhere a mill, a brewery, and a laundry are about to receive visitors with easels, and your purse has never been fuller or further from the ovens.

PEACEKEEPER OCCUPATION

The morning after failure. the occupied neighborhood councils stands beside the checkpoint map and counts what survived. The institutions are imperfect, the loudest hero is already writing a memoir, and peace without foreign control is finally possible if people keep doing the unglamorous work.

text:
GRAIN FUTURES

Tuesday, and bread costs triple what it cost on Sunday. The wheat is right there, green and smug, visible from every window in the village, but Harvest Futures Exchange has sold it forward nine times, so the loaves currently exist in nine contracts and no ovens. The ledger widow taps the glass and asks the field, quite politely, who it belongs to today. Out among the stalks the scarecrow adjusts its sleeve, where somebody has pinned a settlement notice.

choices.text: Countersign the Exchange's tenth forward for a fee

outcomes.text: Mr. Vellum pays in crisp notes that smell faintly of other people's flour. Your signature creates contract number ten, a personal record for a field that has produced zero loaves. The bakery window fills with league faces, not angry, just taking a long careful note. Out in the field the scarecrow shorts your reputation and is up four buttons by dusk.
effects: schedule 5 r18_grain_futures_assembly the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

choices.text: Carry the bread tin door to door

outcomes.text: The tin goes out light and comes back heavy and slightly ashamed of itself. The baker agrees to fire one oven at cost, which is forbidden under clause twelve and delicious under all the others. The ledger widow writes your name under friends, in ink, which is how she proposes. On the evening wind the scarecrow hears the news and quietly opens a long position in you.
effects: schedule 5 r18_grain_futures_assembly the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

outcomes.text: The first door opens on Mr. Vellum, mid audit, and you catch the corner of his satchel in the ribs. The tin is impounded as an unlicensed deposit institution. The village is furious in a way that adds eleven names to the ledger, so the bruise is not for nothing. The scarecrow notes the incident and downgrades the Exchange to hostile with a chance of writs.
effects: schedule 5 r18_grain_futures_assembly the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

choices.text: Demand all nine contracts on the granary table

outcomes.text: Mr. Vellum stacks them, and stacked they stand taller than any bread they describe. Contract four sells wheat to contract six, which leases it back to contract four at interest, and the clerks call this a sophisticated instrument while you call it a field arguing with itself. The registrar accepts your wording into the record, which the Exchange experiences as vandalism. The scarecrow requests copies for its files, meaning its chest.
effects: schedule 5 r18_grain_futures_assembly the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

outcomes.text: The Exchange produces a tenth document instead, which defines produce as a term of art meaning decline to produce. Your complaint is accepted, numbered, and scheduled for a season not found on local calendars. Mr. Vellum expresses sympathy at a fixed rate. In the field, the scarecrow logs your filing as a strong buy signal on frustration.
effects: schedule 5 r18_grain_futures_assembly the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

text:
GRAIN FUTURES

Forty villagers fit in the granary if nobody exhales. The league has gathered to plan for harvest, and the Exchange has sent word that the meeting itself constitutes a market event, attendance to be recorded as insider activity. The ledger widow chairs from the top of a seed crate. Somebody has carried the scarecrow in from the field for consultation, and it presides over the far end of the room, settlement notice still pinned to one sleeve, radiating quiet expertise.

choices.text: Swear the oven oath: every hearth on one rota

outcomes.text: Household by household, the promises stack up: flour from the mill row, fuel from the coppice, Thursday mornings from everyone who has them. The baker cries a little into the register, which smudges nothing important. By vote, the scarecrow is granted observer status and one seat's width of wall. It is the first arrangement anyone here has entered that came with bread attached.
effects: schedule 2 r18_grain_futures_retaliation the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

outcomes.text: The oath gets to the third hearth before the floor gives out under the weight of forty people and their good intentions. You land in the seed cellar still holding somebody's rota sheet. Upstairs, the meeting votes to continue over the hole, which is either resolve or an unwillingness to lose the room. The scarecrow peers down at you with what would be sympathy if it had a face that moved.
effects: schedule 2 r18_grain_futures_retaliation the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

choices.text: Read contract number one aloud, slowly, in full

success.text: It takes two hours with one intermission. By clause thirty the farmers are weeping with laughter, clause fifty defines wheat as any cereal event, and at the definitions of definitions the baker has to be helped outside. A document read aloud in public loses its teeth, and this one is gumming the podium by the end. Even the scarecrow, which has heard the market talk to itself all summer, concedes the market never talks like this.
effects: schedule 2 r18_grain_futures_retaliation the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

fail.text: You lose your place in appendix B and read the same indemnity twice, and the second time everyone hears it properly: it names the granary as forfeitable on demand. The room stops laughing all at once, like weather. The Exchange later thanks you, in writing, for the public disclosure. The scarecrow lowers its price target on the meeting and looks at the door.
effects: schedule 2 r18_grain_futures_retaliation the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

choices.text: Sell tonight's minutes to Mr. Vellum

outcomes.text: He pays by the page, and you are a thorough note taker. The Exchange now knows who promised flour, who promised fuel, and who cried into the register. Your purse is heavier, and the granary door does not open to you in quite the same way. The scarecrow, reviewing the transaction from its wall, moves you from hold to sell.
effects: schedule 2 r18_grain_futures_retaliation the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

text:
GRAIN FUTURES

At dawn the assessors arrive with little orange flags and begin tagging individual stalks of wheat as collateral. The Exchange has declared the village in anticipatory default, which means you have failed to fail in the approved order. Seizure notices go up on the granary doors, one per hinge. The scarecrow now stands in a meadow of tiny flags wearing three of them, having been classified as an improvement to the land.

choices.text: File that nobody may seize the unripe

outcomes.text: The magistrate finds the argument charming and, better, precedented: an old orchard ruling holds that fruit belongs to no ledger until it lets go of the branch. The flags must come down until the wheat consents to be property. Mr. Vellum appeals, is denied, and stands in the lane experiencing weather. The scarecrow keeps its three flags as trophies, or possibly as a hedge.
effects: schedule 3 r18_grain_futures_coalition the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

effects: schedule 3 r18_grain_futures_coalition the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

outcomes.text: The magistrate agrees the wheat is unripe but rules the flags are merely advisory, and advice cannot be seized either. The assessors return with slightly larger flags. Pressure settles over the village like a filing deadline. The scarecrow accepts a fourth flag with the dignity of something that has stopped expecting better from paper.
effects: schedule 3 r18_grain_futures_coalition the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

choices.text: Walk the assessors off the field, flags and all

success.text: It is done gently and by numbers: six farmers per assessor, lifting politely, like moving a table. The flags go into a sack and the assessors go into the lane, and no clause covers being carried. The league disowns the maneuver in public and feeds everyone involved in private. From its post the scarecrow observes that force, properly diversified, is just logistics.
effects: schedule 3 r18_grain_futures_coalition the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

fail.text: The lead assessor turns out to have a baton listed under office supplies. You wake up in the bakery with a lump and a citation for interfering with an inventory. Soldiers from the toll road are seen taking an interest in the district, which nobody ordered and everybody gets. The scarecrow, witness to the whole affair, files it under acts of institution.
effects: schedule 3 r18_grain_futures_coalition the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

choices.text: Set a harvest watch, two neighbors to a field

outcomes.text: The rota fills before supper: shepherds take the north acres, the mill row takes the dark hours, and the ledger widow takes the shift nobody wants, out of principle. Assessors do not tag what is being watched; embarrassment turns out to be a security system. Watch fires make the fields look inhabited, which they now are. The scarecrow gains colleagues for the first time in its career and stands slightly straighter.
effects: schedule 3 r18_grain_futures_coalition the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

outcomes.text: The night watch works until the fog comes in and two watchers flag each other as suspicious persons. In the confusion an assessor tags the well, the bakery cat, and your coat while you are in it. The village laughs about it by morning, which is worth something, though your shoulder disagrees. The scarecrow offers no comment, being professionally acquainted with standing in fog.
effects: schedule 3 r18_grain_futures_coalition the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

text:
GRAIN FUTURES

Word arrives that the next village over is also feeding a contract instead of children, and the one beyond that has been sold forward into next spring. Delegates meet at the mill with their paperwork in wheelbarrows. Laid end to end, the contracts sell the same acres nine times to seven parties, two of which turn out to be each other. The scarecrow attends as regional expert, seated at the map table, and has been issued a pin.

choices.text: Broker the rival claims, take a cut of each

outcomes.text: Each village pays you to prove its contract is the original, which is easy, because they all are. You collect three retainers for one afternoon of nodding. The mill goes quiet when you pass, and the wheelbarrows are angled away from you now. At the map table the scarecrow reclassifies you from participant to instrument.
effects: schedule 4 r18_grain_futures_crisis the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

outcomes.text: Each village pays you to prove its contract is the original, which is easy, because they all are. You collect three retainers for one afternoon of nodding. The mill goes quiet when you pass, and the wheelbarrows are angled away from you now. At the map table the scarecrow reclassifies you from participant to instrument.
effects: schedule 4 r18_grain_futures_crisis the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

choices.text: Pool every receipt into one claim, all names on it

success.text: The wheelbarrows are emptied into one pile and sorted by the ledger widow, who has been waiting her whole life for a pile like this. Together the receipts prove what no single village could: the grain is real, the sales are not, and the arithmetic knows it. Three villages sign one page, which frightens an exchange more than any quantity of pitchforks. The scarecrow pins the page's corner to the table, its first official act.
effects: schedule 4 r18_grain_futures_crisis the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

fail.text: Halfway through sorting, two delegations discover their villages once feuded over a pig and revive the matter at volume. In the scuffle the pile is scattered and you take an elbow meant for history. The claim survives, minus one afternoon and some dignity. The scarecrow holds its pin at the ready, wisely staying out of pork.
effects: schedule 4 r18_grain_futures_crisis the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

choices.text: Draw all nine contracts as a weather map

outcomes.text: You chart the paper as weather: a ridge of obligations over the north acres, a depression of liens circling the mill, a front of settlements due to make landfall at harvest. Pinned to the wall, the whole scheme can be read at a glance, which no contract has ever permitted. The Exchange lodges an objection to its instruments being made legible. The scarecrow studies the map for a long time, then forecasts, with great professional regret, paper hail.
effects: schedule 4 r18_grain_futures_crisis the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

outcomes.text: Your map is accurate, which is the problem: the far village sees it holds the worst position and tries to sell that position to the near one before nightfall. The mill descends into honest bidding. You have invented a smaller, angrier exchange, and it is not even lunchtime. The scarecrow watches the trading with something close to homesickness.
effects: schedule 4 r18_grain_futures_crisis the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

text:
GRAIN FUTURES

The wheat came ripe on a Thursday and the injunction arrived Friday at breakfast: nobody may cut a stalk while ownership is in dispute, and ownership is in dispute nine ways. Meanwhile real weather is assembling on the horizon, black and unpretentious, the kind that does not settle in cash. A courier has pinned a margin call to the scarecrow's chest without making eye contact. The scarecrow, alone in the village, is calm.

choices.text: Cut the wheat together before the sky does

success.text: Every blade in the village is out by noon, and the line moves like one long implement. The injunction requires a stalk to remain uncut, so the ledger widow selects one, fences it with string, and appoints it the dispute. The storm arrives to find the harvest indoors and one legal stalk standing proud in the rain. The scarecrow guards the stalk personally, its position at last fully hedged.
effects: schedule 5 r18_grain_futures_compromise the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

fail.text: The line holds until the rain does not, and the last acres come in by feel and stubbornness through the mud. You strain something that files a complaint every morning after. Most of the grain gets in, wet but real, and the granary smells like a victory that needs drying. The scarecrow, soaked to the armature, holds the margin call above the water line out of pure professionalism.
effects: schedule 5 r18_grain_futures_compromise the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

choices.text: Drag a magistrate out of bed for a hearing

outcomes.text: The magistrate arrives in a nightshirt and robes of office, in that order. You argue the injunction exists to protect the disputed asset, and the storm is about to destroy it, so the injunction now requires the harvest it forbids. The magistrate stares at the sky, then at the paper, and rules that weather lacks standing to take delivery. Cutting is ordered as an act of preservation, and Mr. Vellum is assigned to hold a lantern.
effects: schedule 5 r18_grain_futures_compromise the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

outcomes.text: The magistrate, half asleep, grants a stay of everything, which nobody can interpret before the first thunder. Some cut, some do not, and the lawyers on both sides take careful notes in the rain. The village saves what it can under a legal fog thicker than the real one. On its post the scarecrow rides out both storms, only one of which was scheduled.
effects: schedule 5 r18_grain_futures_compromise the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

choices.text: Sell tarps and lanterns at what the sky will bear

outcomes.text: You had exactly what everyone suddenly needed, at prices that will be quoted back at you for years. The grain that gets in dry gets in under your canvas, and your purse gains a satisfying heft. Nobody haggled, and nobody said thank you either. The scarecrow, observing your lantern trade from the field, marks you to market.
effects: schedule 5 r18_grain_futures_compromise the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

text:
GRAIN FUTURES

Monday, the Exchange proposes settlement in the schoolhouse. The village keeps its granaries, and in return the harvest converts into grain adjacent instruments, redeemable for bread exposure rather than bread. Mr. Vellum presents this as generosity and appears to believe it. Across the table sits the scarecrow, attending as an interested creditor, its margin call answered somehow over the weekend, holding a visibly better position than the Exchange.

choices.text: Invite Vellum to eat one bread exposure now

success.text: He looks at the certificate for a long time. He explains it is not that kind of instrument, and the schoolhouse asks, as one voice, what kind it is, and there is no clause for the silence that follows. The settlement is redrafted by lunch with a delivery requirement, in loaves, of the chewable kind. The scarecrow slides its own certificate across the table to Vellum, who does not laugh either.
effects: schedule 2 r18_grain_futures_reversal the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

fail.text: Vellum eats it. He chews the certificate with the steady face of a man whose employer tests these things, swallows, and pronounces it fungible. The room deflates; you cannot embarrass a man with no clause for shame. The settlement stands another day, and the scarecrow marks the certificate as consumed but not settled.
effects: schedule 2 r18_grain_futures_reversal the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

choices.text: Slip out and audit the Exchange's own book

success.text: The book balances only if nobody ever asks for bread: nine harvests sold, none owned, a standing bet that nobody chews. You copy the page and sell copies at cost to every counterparty on the list, plus a modest finder's fee, which even the honest need. By morning the Exchange's position is public arithmetic, and the ledger guards it had priced are quietly not hired, there being no point guarding a number everyone knows. The league wishes you had asked first, and says so with the door.
effects: schedule 2 r18_grain_futures_reversal the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

fail.text: You find the short position and quietly take the other side of it, which is profitable, and noticed. The Exchange hires ledger guards with actual halberds, a sentence nobody wanted to need. Your winnings arrive in a satchel that ticks like Vellum's. The league says nothing at all, which is louder.
effects: schedule 2 r18_grain_futures_reversal the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

choices.text: Refuse any bread you cannot point at

outcomes.text: The village adopts the pointing test on the spot: if a contract mentions bread, a finger must be able to reach a loaf, or the contract waits outside. It is not sophisticated, which is why it works. Vellum concedes the settlement will need what he calls, with difficulty, physicality. The scarecrow, whose entire career is pointing at a field, is suddenly the most compliant instrument in the room.
effects: schedule 2 r18_grain_futures_reversal the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

outcomes.text: The pointing test holds for an hour, until the Exchange produces a display loaf, baked once, lacquered, and legally pointable. In the ensuing argument the loaf is dropped and dents the floor. You catch a splinter of bread shrapnel above the eyebrow. The scarecrow gazes at the lacquered loaf like a colleague, which in some sense it is.
effects: schedule 2 r18_grain_futures_reversal the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

text:
GRAIN FUTURES

Somebody finally reads the delivery schedule at the back of contract one, and there it is in the smallest print: all nine contracts deliver to the registered occupant of the field. The register lists one occupant, in continuous post since before the Exchange existed. It is the scarecrow. Nine paper harvests converge on one straw creditor, the Exchange's lawyers arrive by dusk to argue that a scarecrow cannot take delivery, and the scarecrow, whose long position on actual grain has just come due, declines through counsel to comment.

choices.text: Offer to manage the straw estate for a fee

outcomes.text: You appoint yourself steward of the scarecrow's affairs before anyone can define the word steward. The fees are excellent, because the estate runs nine harvests deep and its principal cannot object. The village notices you have gone from neighbor to intermediary, and intermediaries eat first and alone. On its post the client watches you with the patience of something that has already read your contract.
effects: schedule 3 r18_grain_futures_legacy the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

effects: schedule 3 r18_grain_futures_legacy the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

choices.text: Pay the field register's back fees yourself

outcomes.text: Forty years of occupancy fees comes to less than you feared; nobody ever billed the scarecrow, on the assumption it could not pay. Paid up, the register is unimpeachable, and the occupant's claim hardens from joke into fact. The ledger widow countersigns as witness, and the mill row stands around the register like it was a crib. Somewhere in the small print, nine harvests quietly change hands to the only party that ever stood in the rain for them.
effects: schedule 3 r18_grain_futures_legacy the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

outcomes.text: The registrar's office has one clerk, one inkwell, and one opinion, and the queue behind you has lawyers in it. In the crush at the counter you take the inkwell to the knuckles, and the village passes a hat when the lawyers offer the clerk money to be slow. The fees get paid, barely, with a queue's worth of grievance attached. The scarecrow's claim survives the afternoon, which is more than your hand can say.
effects: schedule 3 r18_grain_futures_legacy the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

choices.text: Argue the straw's personhood from their own text

outcomes.text: The Exchange's counsel argues a scarecrow lacks personhood, and you table their master agreement, which defines a party as any entity capable of holding a position. The scarecrow has held one all summer, outdoors, unpaid, in the rain. The magistrate rules for the straw and orders delivery in grain, actual grain, the kind with weight. Counsel asks for time to consult, and the magistrate observes that the field is right there.
effects: schedule 3 r18_grain_futures_legacy the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

outcomes.text: The magistrate accepts that the scarecrow is a party, then appoints the Exchange as its guardian, on the grounds that straw requires representation and the Exchange was standing nearest. It is the wrong answer arrived at through your own reasoning, which stings twice. Appeal papers are filed before supper. The scarecrow, informed it now has a guardian, develops the first opinion of its career, and it is not printable.
effects: schedule 3 r18_grain_futures_legacy the wheat is fine, the paperwork is starving

text:
GRAIN FUTURES

Harvest home, and the granaries hold grain instead of liens. The scarecrow has been carried to a new post on the granary roof and formally promoted to oracle, its sleeve pinned with the first honest instrument the village ever issued: one contract, one loaf, deliverable to anyone who can point at it. The Exchange's leftover forms have been repurposed, the indemnity schedule ruling itself nicely into a rota sheet. Mr. Vellum visits monthly now, off the books, to ask the roof what the wind says about spring.

choices.text: Charter the bread rota on the old indemnity forms

outcomes.text: The forms take the ink beautifully; whoever designed them for seizure gave them generous margins. Each hearth signs a line that once threatened it, and the words whereas and hereby now announce whose turn it is to bake. The first document in village memory to feed people is a repurposed threat, which everyone agrees is the correct use of legal stationery. Above the door the oracle rustles its sleeve, going long on the village at any price.

The chartering supper doubles as a signing and triples as an argument about Thursdays. You are wedged against the oven when the dispute peaks and come away lightly toasted down one side. The forms get signed anyway, greasy and binding. The oracle on the roof declines to arbitrate Thursdays, citing a conflict of interest with weather.

choices.text: Ask the oracle to set the planting forecast

success.text: The reply comes by pinned note, in the market's own grammar: long wheat, short paper, scattered obligations clearing by noon. Translated by the ledger widow, it means plant everything and answer letters slowly. The far villages send for copies and pay in seed. It is the first forecast in the region's history issued by a party with no position it did not disclose.

fail.text: The forecast is accurate and nobody understands it: the note says butter at the strike, whatever that costs, and half the village plants barley in a panic. The magistrate asks, formally, whether the oracle is regulated, and nobody knows what it would even be regulated as. The oracle adds a disclaimer to its sleeve, its first clause. Everyone pretends this is not how the Exchange started.

choices.text: Charge distant traders for time with the oracle

outcomes.text: They come in polished boots to consult the roof, and you take a coin at the ladder. The oracle tells every one of them the same thing, which is that the grain is downstairs and not for sale, and they pay again to hear it twice. It is the most honest booth in the region and you are its least honest part. At month's end the oracle pins a note to your share: fee schedule noted, position unchanged.

text:
PUBLIC TRANSIT

At 4:58 the new timetable goes up in the depot break room, laminated while still warm. RoadChoice Conglomerate has deleted every minute that fails to pay for itself, which turns out to be most of them: the 5:10 through the mill district now officially never happened. The driver of the 9 copies the missing times onto the back of his hand before anyone can stop him. The dispatcher narrates all of this aloud, present tense, to nobody in particular.

choices.text: Appeal the deletion of the 5:10 on official paper

outcomes.text: The form exists: Request for Reinstatement of a Discontinued Minute, in triplicate, blue ink only. A clerk stamps it PENDING, and pending, it turns out, is a legal afterlife: a pending minute may be operated at the operator's own risk until reviewed, and reviews are not profitable either. The nurse who rides the 5:10 reads the stamp twice and laughs like a drain. The dispatcher narrates the stamp landing, and the break room gives it a standing ovation.
effects: schedule 3 r18_public_transit_assembly The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

The nurse who rides the 5:10 reads the stamp twice and laughs like a drain. The dispatcher narrates the stamp landing, and the break room gives it a standing ovation.

outcomes.text: The appeal is refused on standing: you cannot represent a minute without holding a ticket for it, and tickets are not sold for minutes that do not exist. The clerk is very sorry, in the way of a man reading sorry off a card. Outside, the queue for the 5:10 waits anyway, out of habit and principle, in that order.
effects: schedule 3 r18_public_transit_assembly The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

choices.text: Sell RoadChoice your notes on who still waits where

outcomes.text: A man in a grey coat counts out cash for your list of stops where crowds still gather at dawn. He calls it optimization data; the drivers call it a map of whom to abandon next, and they say so politely, to your face, at every shift change from now on. The next timetable is thinner. Your wallet is not.
effects: schedule 3 r18_public_transit_assembly The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

choices.text: Put money in the diesel tin and ride the 5:10 anyway

outcomes.text: The tin accepts your coins with the dignity of a national bank. At 5:10 exactly the unlisted bus pulls out, full, on time to a schedule that denies it exists, and the driver of the 4 nods at you once, which witnesses agree has never happened before. On the back of the laminated timetable someone has pencilled the first ghost minute. The dispatcher logs the departure as: nothing leaves the depot, punctually.
effects: schedule 3 r18_public_transit_assembly The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

outcomes.text: The 5:10's engine has been cold since the deletion, and cold engines hold grudges. You crank it while the driver of the 9 shouts encouragement that is mostly diagnosis, and the folding door takes a piece of your knuckle as a boarding fee. The bus runs, the queue cheers in a tone the authorities would call unrest. You bleed quietly in the second seat, officially not aboard, officially not bleeding.
effects: schedule 3 r18_public_transit_assembly The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

text:
PUBLIC TRANSIT

Midnight in the break room: seventeen people, one kettle, and the laminated timetable turned to face the wall in disgrace. On its back, in pencil, a second schedule is growing, every deleted minute rewritten in the handwriting of whoever missed it most. The driver of the 9 and the driver of the 4 are arguing over whose route loses money more beautifully. The dispatcher calls the meeting to order by narrating it to order.

choices.text: Attend as RoadChoice's paid efficiency witness

outcomes.text: RoadChoice pays handsomely for an observer, and you observe. Your report states that morale is low, attendance is high, and the kettle is load bearing, all of which is true and none of which helps them. The meeting votes to stop asking nicely, which your report files under other. The drivers find the stipend stub in your coat within the week; nobody says anything, but the pencil schedule acquires a new column titled seen by, and you are in it.
effects: schedule 4 r18_public_transit_retaliation The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

choices.text: Take Tuesdays on the rota of unprofitable minutes

outcomes.text: Your name goes on the rota between a retired mechanic and a woman who once drove a tank, professionally, she insists. Tuesdays are yours now: the 4:40 over the bridge, the 6:05 nobody rides but everyone needs known about. The driver of the 4 teaches you the wave that means inspector out. The pencil column gets your Tuesdays in your own handwriting, which is how a timetable becomes a promise.
effects: schedule 4 r18_public_transit_retaliation The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

outcomes.text: You volunteer for the 4:40 over the bridge before anyone mentions the heater died with the route. By the far bank you cannot feel your fingers, and the handbrake takes the skin off two of them as a formality. Seventeen people saw you sign up. Seventeen people bring you gloves over the next three days, one glove at a time, so nobody can be accused of generosity.
effects: schedule 4 r18_public_transit_retaliation The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

choices.text: Forge a supplemental annex in RoadChoice's own font

success.text: The annex is a masterpiece of institutional beige: Schedule B, Provisional Community Minutes, formatted so exactly in RoadChoice's house style that it looks bored of itself. Posted beside the laminated original, it is obeyed by two inspectors, one courier, and eventually a regional manager who signs it to be safe. The pencil minutes now have a typeface. The dispatcher narrates the moment a forgery becomes filing.
effects: schedule 4 r18_public_transit_retaliation The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

fail.text: Your annex is perfect except for the kerning, and RoadChoice employs a man whose entire job is kerning. The document is declared counterfeit with a satisfaction that borders on the romantic, and the break room loses its photocopier privileges. The pencil schedule goes back to pencil. It seems relieved, honestly.
effects: schedule 4 r18_public_transit_retaliation The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

text:
PUBLIC TRANSIT

Two inspectors arrive at dawn with white gloves and a Compliance Verification Team lanyard each. They lift the laminated timetable off the wall for authentication, pencil side carefully unphotographed, while outside a tow rig backs toward Doris, the depot's spare bus and only unanimously loved employee. The driver of the 9 stands very still, the way a man stands when he is choosing his next decade. The dispatcher narrates the raid in real time, which the inspectors find deeply unsettling.

choices.text: Post people at the dead stops holding times aloft

outcomes.text: By noon every deleted stop has a person holding cardboard with a time on it, a timetable made of neighbors, updated by shouting. The 5:10 navigates by them like a coastline. RoadChoice cannot confiscate a schedule that is also forty citizens standing where they are legally allowed to stand. The inspectors photograph everyone and are waved at.
effects: schedule 5 r18_public_transit_coalition The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

outcomes.text: Sleet arrives at the same time as your shift with the cardboard. Four hours in, your sign reads 6:05 and your hands read nothing at all, and a kind stranger has to pry the timetable out of your fist at the end. The 6:05 came. You are told this later, indoors, wrapped around a mug you cannot yet feel.
effects: schedule 5 r18_public_transit_coalition The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

choices.text: Demand a chain of custody receipt for the timetable

outcomes.text: The inspectors, cornered by procedure, itemize their seizure: one laminated timetable, annotations reverse, condition to be preserved. That last clause is yours, dictated slowly, and it means RoadChoice is now legally responsible for protecting the pencil minutes it came to destroy. Evidence must be kept intact; the ghost schedule has acquired a bodyguard with a legal department. The dispatcher requests a copy of the receipt for the archive, meaning the biscuit tin.
effects: schedule 5 r18_public_transit_coalition The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

outcomes.text: The receipt reads: one sheet, laminated, contents unverified. Unverified is a load bearing word, you learn, because unverified contents can be disposed of after thirty days without ceremony. Your objection is noted, in the sense that a man says noted while walking away. The wall keeps the timetable's clean rectangle of unfaded paint, like a held breath.
effects: schedule 5 r18_public_transit_coalition The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

choices.text: Put yourself between Doris and the tow rig

success.text: You stand in the tow arc with your arms folded, doing sums out loud: cost of the rig per hour, cost of an incident report, cost of everyone watching. The tow man consults his own internal timetable and concludes this minute is not profitable. He leaves with Doris still on her bricks and his dignity mostly intact. The drivers thank you and also, quietly, resent the theatrics; there was a rota for standing in front of Doris, and you jumped it.
effects: schedule 5 r18_public_transit_coalition The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

fail.text: The tow crew has brought private security, and private security has brought opinions. You are removed from the tow arc in the manner of furniture, twice, and the second landing is on the fuel cap. Doris goes up the ramp with her dignity intact, which is more than can be said for anyone else present, while the dispatcher narrates her exit like a state funeral. The depot files you under injured and Doris under equipment, and only one of those feels temporary.
effects: schedule 5 r18_public_transit_coalition The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

text:
PUBLIC TRANSIT

A bakery cart is parked at the depot gate, which is how the drivers learn their ghost minutes have customers. Night nurses, market gardeners, a choir with rehearsals across town: photocopies of the pencil schedule are circulating folded into hymn books and invoice pads. The break room now hosts strangers who know the kettle rules without being told. The driver of the 4 pretends to hate this and has memorized every newcomer's stop.

choices.text: Print the ghost minutes in the parish letter as a poem

outcomes.text: The parish letter runs it under Times Our Village Is Officially Not Moving, forty lines, unrhymed, devastating. Legally it is literature, and literature cannot be discontinued for underperformance, a precedent RoadChoice's lawyers confirm in a memo they clearly hated writing. The bakery schedules its bread to the third stanza. The pencil timetable has been published, which is one step from beloved.
effects: schedule 2 r18_public_transit_crisis The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

outcomes.text: RoadChoice's lawyers respond that the poem infringes the distinctive look and feel of a timetable, an argument so stupid it succeeds provisionally. The parish letter prints a retraction in verse, which everyone memorizes out of spite, which was possibly the plan. Meanwhile the pencil schedule goes back under the biscuit tin. Poetry, the dispatcher notes, was always a risky sector.
effects: schedule 2 r18_public_transit_crisis The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

choices.text: Charge the newcomers a finder's fee for the times

outcomes.text: You set up near the gate with the confidence of a man selling weather. A coin per minute, times recited from memory, and the newcomers pay because the alternative is standing in the dark doing arithmetic about despair. It works. It keeps working. The dispatcher narrates each of your transactions in the tone reserved for wildlife documentaries, and the pencil schedule gains an asterisk beside every minute you have retailed.
effects: schedule 2 r18_public_transit_crisis The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

choices.text: Drive the dawn loop for the market gardeners

success.text: The dawn loop runs heavy with crates and light on conversation, which suits everyone aboard. Cabbages ride where commuters were deleted, and the market opens stocked for the first time since the cuts. The gardeners pay in produce, the kettle gains a soup rota, and the pencil schedule acquires a minute with a carrot drawn beside it.
effects: schedule 2 r18_public_transit_crisis The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

fail.text: A crate of winter beets has its own ideas on the corner by the weighbridge, and you catch the whole cargo with your ribs. The gardeners are apologetic and efficient, in that order, and you finish the route taped up with the stoicism of a man who has become infrastructure. The market gets its produce. You get soup rights for life, which, breathing carefully, you cannot yet enjoy.
effects: schedule 2 r18_public_transit_crisis The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

text:
PUBLIC TRANSIT

One sheet arrives by courier, laminated, mostly white. The new official timetable lists a single minute: the 8:44 express, profitable, punctual, alone on the page like a survivor photograph. Everything else is a footnote explaining that service concepts are being reimagined toward flexibility. The break room reads it in silence, and then the driver of the 9 laughs so hard he has to sit on the floor.

choices.text: Buy the decommissioned buses at scrap price

outcomes.text: You bid scrap price for three retired buses and win, because nobody at RoadChoice can imagine wanting one. Within a month you are leasing them back to the very ghost runs they were deleted from, at rates the drivers pay because they must. It is legal, tidy, and profitable, three words that have started to sound like one word. The dispatcher narrates your invoices now, slowly, so everyone can hear the punctuation.
effects: schedule 3 r18_public_transit_compromise The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

choices.text: Run every deleted minute in a single day

success.text: The pencil schedule runs complete for one whole day, every deleted minute operated in order, unlisted and immaculate. The town moves like it remembers itself: nurses, gardeners, the choir arriving somewhere on time in full gowns. RoadChoice's monitoring software reports zero passengers moved, and the software is, in its own terms, correct. The 8:44 express runs too, on schedule, empty as a promise.
effects: schedule 3 r18_public_transit_compromise The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

fail.text: By the fourteenth consecutive minute your hands are shaking on the wheel, and someone wisely swaps you out at the bridge. You spend the afternoon being fed soup and watching the rest of the day succeed without you, which stings more than the sprained wrist does. The town moved. The pencil schedule held. You are ordered to rest by four people who have never once rested.
effects: schedule 3 r18_public_transit_compromise The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

choices.text: Argue one minute cannot legally constitute a table

outcomes.text: Your filing is short: a timetable is, statutorily, a table of times, plural, and a table with one entry is merely a fact with margins. The tribunal, delighted by anything resembling a real question, agrees. RoadChoice is ordered to restore a second minute and, in a sulk visible from space, schedules the 8:45. Two minutes is a table; a table can be appealed; the door is now open exactly one minute wide, which is all the pencil ever needed.
effects: schedule 3 r18_public_transit_compromise The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

outcomes.text: The tribunal rules that a single listed minute is not a table but a declaration, and declarations fall outside transit regulation entirely. RoadChoice has been accidentally deregulated by your own filing, a result their lawyers frame and hang. The dispatcher narrates your walk back from the courthouse with unusual gentleness. The pencil schedule does not judge you. It has margins for this.
effects: schedule 3 r18_public_transit_compromise The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

text:
PUBLIC TRANSIT

A negotiator in a soft grey suit sets a briefcase on the break room table, between the kettle and the biscuit tin, which everyone silently notes is neutral territory. RoadChoice will restore six minutes, laminated and official, if the ghost runs cease and the pencil schedule is surrendered for certified destruction. The nurse from the 5:10 asks which six, and the negotiator says that would be premature. The dispatcher narrates the pause that follows in full.

choices.text: Keep the pencil column, decline the printed one

outcomes.text: The room does not vote so much as fail to blink. The negotiator packs the briefcase with the care of a man reburying something, and the pencil schedule stays under the biscuit tin, guarded by custom and crumbs. Six official minutes were on offer; the room preferred all of its own. On his way out, the negotiator checks the pencil column for his street. Everyone sees him do it.
effects: schedule 4 r18_public_transit_reversal The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

outcomes.text: The room does not vote so much as fail to blink. The negotiator packs the briefcase with the care of a man reburying something, and the pencil schedule stays under the biscuit tin, guarded by custom and crumbs. Six official minutes were on offer; the room preferred all of its own. On his way out, the negotiator checks the pencil column for his street. Everyone sees him do it.
effects: schedule 4 r18_public_transit_reversal The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

outcomes.text: Negotiations continue for eleven hours because nobody will adjourn first, and the break room heater surrendered years ago. You give up your chair to the nurse at hour six and stand until your back files a grievance of its own. The offer dies of exhaustion around midnight. So, very nearly, do you, into the good chair, which the driver of the 4 lets you have without comment, once.
effects: schedule 4 r18_public_transit_reversal The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

choices.text: Accept, but let the depot define the word cease

success.text: You sign gladly, having first defined cease per RoadChoice's own glossary, where discontinued services pending review remain operable at operator risk. The ghost runs therefore cease into exactly what they already were, with the added shine of a settlement protecting them. The negotiator reads the definitions page twice and ages visibly between readings. The laminated and pencil schedules now cite each other, like colleagues.
effects: schedule 4 r18_public_transit_reversal The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

fail.text: Their lawyer accepts your definitions and adds one comma, and you sign while the kettle distracts everyone by boiling. The comma turns operator risk into operator liability, and now every ghost minute runs with its own insurance premium attached. The runs continue; the drivers pay for the privilege in paperwork. The negotiator shakes your hand with genuine warmth, which is somehow the worst part.
effects: schedule 4 r18_public_transit_reversal The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

choices.text: Tail the grey suit to see how he gets home

success.text: He walks past the executive car park, checks both ways, and boards the unlisted 5:10 with the practiced shame of a regular. You sit beside him and say nothing, which costs him more than words would have. By the bridge he has offered a month of diesel money for discretion; by the depot he has offered to lose the destruction clause entirely. You accept both. The drivers, hearing only that you followed a man for money, dock your kettle privileges on principle.
effects: schedule 4 r18_public_transit_reversal The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

fail.text: You sell the sighting to a rival department inside RoadChoice, because conglomerates contain their own weather. The negotiator is transferred somewhere with fewer buses to be seen on, and his replacement has never boarded anything but a plane. The depot hears what you did and what it paid, in that order. The 5:10 still stops for you. It is, everyone agrees, better than you deserve.
effects: schedule 4 r18_public_transit_reversal The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

text:
PUBLIC TRANSIT

Quarterly figures leak on a Thursday, and the dispatcher reads them aloud like banns. The 8:44 express, RoadChoice's last profitable minute, has averaged zero riders since the cuts: it turns out the profitable minutes were only ever profitable because the unprofitable ones delivered people to them. Meanwhile every pencil minute runs full. In the back row of the 5:10, the regional auditor sits with his collar up, hoping very much not to be narrated.

choices.text: File the pencil schedule as the document of record

outcomes.text: The tribunal examines both timetables and rules on plain evidence: the laminated sheet describes no transit, the pencil column describes all of it, therefore the pencil column is the timetable within the meaning of the act. Handwriting becomes precedent. RoadChoice must now petition the depot for amendments, on the correct form, in blue ink, and the drivers have opinions about processing times. The dispatcher narrates the stamping for the better part of a minute.
effects: schedule 5 r18_public_transit_legacy The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

outcomes.text: The tribunal accepts the pencil schedule into evidence and immediately seals it, because accurate documents about infrastructure are, apparently, sensitive. The truth is now official and unreadable, filed in a basement with the other victories. The drivers keep running the minutes from memory, which cannot be sealed. The clean rectangle on the break room wall gets a frame anyway, empty, on principle.
effects: schedule 5 r18_public_transit_legacy The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

choices.text: License the missing minutes back to RoadChoice

outcomes.text: RoadChoice buys back its own deleted minutes at a markup, rebrands them Community Journey Moments, and pays you a finder's percentage for the introduction. You have sold the timetable to the people who threw it away, which even the dispatcher narrates twice to be sure. The ghost runs continue exactly as before, now with branded seat covers nobody asked for. The money is real. The seat covers itch.
effects: schedule 5 r18_public_transit_legacy The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

choices.text: Buy fares for the 8:44 so it stops riding alone

outcomes.text: You put coins in the diesel tin under a new label, fares for the lonely express, and at 8:44 the ghost crews board their rival minute together, off duty, unpaid, unannounced. The driver of the 8:44, who has completed four hundred empty runs with perfect punctuality, grips the wheel and says nothing for three stops. The auditor pays his own fare, in the open, in front of witnesses. The pencil schedule adds the 8:44 to its column, adopted, and the laminated sheet is finally, in one small square, correct.
effects: schedule 5 r18_public_transit_legacy The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

outcomes.text: Word spreads faster than seating: the 8:44 boards forty three passengers into a bus built for twelve, and the crush at the bridge stop introduces your elbow to the fare box, hard. The driver of the 8:44 weeps anyway, over the intercom, at length. It is the fullest profitable minute in RoadChoice history, and the press release writes itself; you, wedged and wheezing, are technically ridership. The town calls it a triumph. Your elbow files a minority opinion.
effects: schedule 5 r18_public_transit_legacy The 5:10 still runs. Officially it never did.

text:
PUBLIC TRANSIT

Six months on, the break room wall holds one timetable again: relaminated, both sides showing, the printed minutes and the pencil minutes sealed together under the same plastic. Newcomers are made to study it before they are told the kettle rules. Doris sits on her bricks by the gate with a fresh coat of paint and the air of a bus with tenure. The dispatcher has begun narrating in the past tense, which everyone understands to mean the story held.

choices.text: Sell the ghost network's route maps to a security firm

outcomes.text: A contractor pays extremely well for coverage maps of who moves where and when, for reasons described as community safety logistics. Their patrol vans appear within the month, run eleven minutes late, and idle at stops the pencil schedule retired years ago. The drivers reroute around them without a meeting, the way water reroutes. Your money spends fine. The kettle, whenever you approach it, has always just boiled dry.

Their patrol vans appear within the month, run eleven minutes late, and idle at stops the pencil schedule retired years ago. The drivers reroute around them without a meeting, the way water reroutes. Your money spends fine. The kettle, whenever you approach it, has always just boiled dry.

choices.text: Take the empty seat at the dispatch desk

outcomes.text: The dispatch chair has been vacant on Sundays for as long as anyone can prove, and you take it, headset and all. Your first act is reading the whole timetable aloud, both columns, no difference in your voice between the printed minutes and the pencilled ones, because there is no longer a difference to perform. The patrol requests that come in get filed under pending, indefinitely, per tradition. The dispatcher, off duty in the good chair, does not correct you once, which is the highest honor the depot awards.

Your first act is reading the whole timetable aloud, both columns, no difference in your voice between the printed minutes and the pencilled ones, because there is no longer a difference to perform. The patrol requests that come in get filed under pending, indefinitely, per tradition. The dispatcher, off duty in the good chair, does not correct you once, which is the highest honor the depot awards.

Your first Sunday on dispatch, the ancient headset cord snags a passing trolley and takes a notch out of your ear, and the first aid kit turns out to date from the previous conglomerate. You bleed thinly on the log book and keep announcing departures, because the schedule does not pause for staffing incidents. The 5:10 goes out on time. Your entry in the log reads: minor incident, dispatch continued, kettle deployed.

choices.text: Nominate the timetable for heritage protection

success.text: The heritage board certifies the relaminated timetable as a document of local significance, protected from removal, alteration, or improvement. Improvement is the load bearing word: RoadChoice's next efficiency review arrives, reads the plaque, and files itself away unopened. The pencil minutes are now the law's problem to protect, a plot twist the pencil accepts modestly. Visitors are shown the wall. The kettle is mentioned on the plaque, as context.

fail.text: Heritage status arrives with conditions: the timetable may never be amended, not even in pencil, not even when the bridge works change the 4:40. The depot now maintains a third schedule, kept in a drawer, listing corrections to the protected schedule that lists corrections to the official one. The dispatcher narrates the drawer opening with a certain weariness. Somewhere, a filing cabinet is very happy.

text:
SHIP MUTINY

Six days out, the second cook pries a swollen book from under the salt pork: the ship's articles, pages still uncut. Captain Final Word has sworn by them at every decree, including this morning's ruling that complaints will be deducted from rations. Clause nine, the cook discovers, says rations may be changed only by vote of the mess. Also, someone has nailed a petition to the mainmast, in which the mainmast requests recognition as middle management, and nobody will admit whose handwriting it is.

choices.text: Sell the captain a first look at his own articles

outcomes.text: He pays for the book and then, at a rate you invent on the spot, for your discretion. The clerk files both sums under hull maintenance without meeting your eye. By supper the galley knows exactly what discretion cost, and prices theirs accordingly. On the way out you salute the mainmast, whose petition has gained a footnote in your hand: management pays promptly.
effects: schedule 4 r18_ship_mutiny_assembly The mast has a petition and the storm has counsel

outcomes.text: He pays for the book and then, at a rate you invent on the spot, for your discretion. The clerk files both sums under hull maintenance without meeting your eye. By supper the galley knows exactly what discretion cost, and prices theirs accordingly. On the way out you salute the mainmast, whose petition has gained a footnote in your hand: management pays promptly.
effects: schedule 4 r18_ship_mutiny_assembly The mast has a petition and the storm has counsel

choices.text: Read clause nine aloud over the evening soup

success.text: You stand on the bench and give clause nine the delivery it deserves, pausing where the applause wants to live. By the second reading the stokers are mouthing along, and the cook is bookmarking clauses like a woman planning a menu. The mainmast creaks once at the word management, which everyone agrees to note and nobody agrees to interpret.
effects: schedule 4 r18_ship_mutiny_assembly The mast has a petition and the storm has counsel

fail.text: You are three words in when the captain's steward removes the bench from under you as a matter of policy. You finish the clause from the floor, which the galley agrees showed commitment. The cook keeps the book, the crews keep the memory, and your elbow keeps a souvenir.

choices.text: Present clause nine to the captain through channels

outcomes.text: You request an audience, cite the clause, and lay the book on his desk open to the page, spine flat, exhibit style. Captain Final Word swears by the articles again, and this time you watch him hear himself do it. Rations are restored pending review, which is the sound a door makes when it has not decided whether it is open.

outcomes.text: The clerk arrives before your second sentence, carrying the appendix of definitions, wherein the mess is defined as the officers' dining room. The officers vote to keep eating well, unanimously, with gravy. You leave with the book and a new respect for whoever wrote that appendix, which turns out to matter later.

text:
SHIP MUTINY

The first full reading of the articles convenes in the engine room between watches, lit by boiler glow and chaired by whoever holds the book. The clerk attends to observe, which he does the way a hawk observes a field. Item four on the agenda is the mainmast's petition, entered into the minutes for morale and seconded by a creak that arrives suspiciously on cue. Outside, the storm glass has begun a long private argument with the horizon.

choices.text: Chair the reading and give every clause its turn

success.text: You keep the speakers to order and the order to purpose, and clauses nobody has heard in years stand up and stretch. The stokers learn they are owed shore leave in triplicate, the cook learns the galley budget has a floor, and the minutes record the mast's petition as pending rather than funny. Pending, the crews agree, is a promotion.
effects: schedule 5 r18_ship_mutiny_retaliation The mast has a petition and the storm has counsel

fail.text: Chairing goes well until the question of who speaks for the coal trimmers, which is settled by the traditional method of a shoved bench in the dark. You take a corner of the boiler housing with your ribs and rule the shove out of order from the floor. The reading survives, and so do you, technically.
effects: schedule 5 r18_ship_mutiny_retaliation The mast has a petition and the storm has counsel

choices.text: Trade the clerk definition for definition

outcomes.text: You concede him the meaning of promptly, he concedes you the meaning of provisions, and the room watches the exchange like a fencing final. By the end you have traded three small words for the single load bearing one: crew. The clerk closes his book with the expression of a man who has enjoyed losing to a professional, and the minutes gain a definitions annex the captain has never read either.

outcomes.text: He lets you build a beautiful argument on appendix six, then mentions that appendix six was repealed the year the ship was launched. The room exhales as one disappointed lung. You surrender the point with a bow, which salvages style, though the minutes record the bow and not the argument.

choices.text: Copy the articles out and sell them by the clause

outcomes.text: You price clause nine like bread and the shore leave clauses like cake, and by the end of the watch you have sold the same book thirty times in pieces. The definitions page goes at a premium to a stoker who frames it. The mainmast receives a complimentary copy nailed at eye level, which for a mast is every level, and the minutes note the gesture as goodwill, sincerity unaudited.

text:
SHIP MUTINY

Decree forty four appears at dawn, prohibiting readings, gatherings, and quorums of any size, including one. It also orders the petition pried off the mainmast, which the carpenter does under protest, leaving a nail hole that the articles classify as an unreported structural modification. The mainmast, in other words, now has a grievance of its own, and the clerk is the one who has to log it.

choices.text: Challenge the decree with the clause it quotes

success.text: Decree forty four cites clause twelve for its authority, and clause twelve requires decrees to be posted three days before taking effect. You post the objection beside the decree and invite the crew to obey both, carefully, in order. For three days the ship runs on tea and technicality, and on the fourth the decree quietly falls overboard, unwitnessed, mourned by no one.
effects: schedule 2 r18_ship_mutiny_coalition The mast has a petition and the storm has counsel

fail.text: The clerk produces the emergency provisions, in which emergency is defined as any circumstance the captain designates in writing. The captain designates Tuesday. You concede the day but request the designation in triplicate, and his signature wobbles on the third copy, which everyone pretends not to see.
effects: schedule 2 r18_ship_mutiny_coalition The mast has a petition and the storm has counsel

choices.text: Slip into the chart room while the officers dine

success.text: The navigation charts are honest the way diaries are honest. Every course for two years bends away from any port with an inspector, a creditor, or a functioning post office. You copy the pattern onto one page and pin it to the mast, where it explains two years of odd landfalls at a glance, and a creditor's agent at the next anchorage pays you a modest sum for confirming what his ledgers suspected. The crews appreciate the truth, and note that you invoiced it.
effects: schedule 2 r18_ship_mutiny_coalition The mast has a petition and the storm has counsel

fail.text: Beside the charts you find the captain's private ledger, and beside your scruples you find a price for one page of it. A cargo agent ashore pays handsomely by lamplight. The crews notice only that you went alone and came back smiling, and on this ship, lately, arithmetic like that gets done fast.
effects: schedule 2 r18_ship_mutiny_coalition The mast has a petition and the storm has counsel

choices.text: Claim the hammer along with everyone else

outcomes.text: The captain demands the name of whoever nailed the petition, and by muster's end forty one sailors have confessed to owning the hammer. Clause twenty caps total fines at the value of the instrument, and the instrument is appraised, by the carpenter, at one biscuit. The fine is paid in crumbs, formally, on a plate.

outcomes.text: You confess loudest and first, which is how you win the honor of the mainmast watch in the rain while the others draw lots indoors. The mast creaks all night like a colleague settling into a new office. By morning you are soaked, feverish at the edges, and listed in its grievance as a sympathetic party.

text:
SHIP MUTINY

By the second week the mainmast holds more paper than the clerk's desk: grievances, rota proposals, one poem. The deck watch wants in with the galley and engine crews but wants terms, the purser hints he could be neutral at the right price, and the storm glass has stopped arguing with the horizon and started packing. Somebody has chalked IN TRAY on the mast's north side. Nobody has chalked OUT.

choices.text: Broker the deck watch's entry and skim both sides

outcomes.text: You charge the deck watch for the introduction and the galley for the guarantee, and the fee for keeping the two fees confidential is itself a third fee. Everyone gets what they paid for, which is you, briefly. The purser watches the whole transaction with the professional warmth of one mirror facing another, and the mast acquires a new pinned note in your hand: commission structure, draft one.

Fed stokers turn out to be persuasive stokers, and the deck watch signs on over bowls, terms forgotten, spoons unanimous. The mast's in tray gains its first item marked resolved, pinned dead center by the cook, where the mast can keep an eye on it.

choices.text: Draft one watch bill both crews can sign

outcomes.text: You write a watch bill so balanced that reading it feels like standing amidships. Deck takes the mast watch, engine takes the glass, galley feeds both, and command of the schedule belongs to the schedule. The clerk reviews it for traps, finds his own favorite semicolon deployed against him, and initials the margin with what can only be called respect.

You have written the most attentive document ever to change nothing, and the deck watch wants a word about whose clever idea it was.

text:
SHIP MUTINY

The storm announces itself in writing: an albatross delivers a sodden envelope, stamped by the System's maritime registry, containing an amicus brief in which the weather petitions to be consulted on any sailing plan intending to occur inside it. Captain Final Word orders full sail regardless, and the clerk has already ruled the weather moderate, since the articles define severe weather by readings from three storm glasses and the ship carries two. The mainmast's in tray is taking spray.

choices.text: Reef the sails as one crew, on counsel's advice

outcomes.text: Every hand that can reach canvas reaches it at once, and the sails come in with the calm of a decision already made elsewhere. When the captain demands names, he is handed the storm's brief, cited as instructing counsel. The ship steadies, the crews exhale together, and the mast stands a little straighter, as middle management will when the department performs.
effects: schedule 4 r18_ship_mutiny_compromise The mast has a petition and the storm has counsel

outcomes.text: The reefing succeeds, but the sea files an objection through your handhold, and you meet a spar the way an argument meets a deadline. The cook straps your shoulder and remarks that the storm should tip you for the assist. The sails are in, the ship is safer, and you are one bruise closer to the weather's side of the case.
effects: schedule 4 r18_ship_mutiny_compromise The mast has a petition and the storm has counsel

choices.text: Move the storm's brief into the ship's record

success.text: You read the brief aloud at the mast, and it is a beautiful piece of work: the weather cites the articles' own weather annex, notes its longstanding professional relationship with the vessel, and seconds, in passing, the mainmast's structural concerns. Entered into the record, it cannot be unentered. The clerk copies it in silence, and his pen slows at the second page, where the storm has used his own citation format, correctly.
effects: schedule 4 r18_ship_mutiny_compromise The mast has a petition and the storm has counsel

fail.text: The clerk rules the albatross an unlicensed courier and the brief therefore undelivered, whatever your hands are visibly holding. The storm takes the ruling personally, as filers do. Delivery is reattempted overnight against the hull, repeatedly, and the record is amended to say received.
effects: schedule 4 r18_ship_mutiny_compromise The mast has a petition and the storm has counsel

choices.text: Sell the captain the third storm glass he lacks

outcomes.text: You have owned a third storm glass since the definitions ruling, which is either foresight or authorship, and at these prices you decline to specify. The captain pays in coin, the clerk logs it as safety equipment, and the instrument immediately reads what the other two read: severe. The crews do the arithmetic on when you bought it, and the mast's in tray receives a note about you, unsigned, weatherproofed.

text:
SHIP MUTINY

Negotiations convene at the mainmast, which chairs by the simple precedent of standing where the meeting is. Captain Final Word offers restored rations and a rewritten decree in exchange for one concession: withdrawal, with prejudice, of the mast's petition. The storm attends without invitation, holding the ship at a steady heel like a bailiff keeping order. The clerk has brought two inkwells, which the cook says is how you know it is serious.

choices.text: Accept his terms and then define his terms

outcomes.text: You accept everything, gratefully, pending definitions. Restored proves to mean restored to the levels in clause nine, rewritten proves to mean ratified by the reading, and withdrawal proves to mean transferred, since the articles nowhere provide for a petition's destruction, only its reassignment. The mast's petition is reassigned to the minutes, where it becomes item four, permanent. The captain signs a surrender arranged alphabetically as a victory.
effects: schedule 5 r18_ship_mutiny_reversal The mast has a petition and the storm has counsel

outcomes.text: The clerk was waiting for you at the definitions, the way a good doorman waits at the only door. Concession, he notes, is defined in the appendix as final. You save the rations and lose the petition, and the mast takes the news with one long creak that the minutes record, at the cook's insistence, as dissent.
effects: schedule 5 r18_ship_mutiny_reversal The mast has a petition and the storm has counsel

choices.text: Wedge the wheelhouse shut until he signs

success.text: The wedge is oak, the door is teak, and the captain's schedule loses the argument with both. He signs at the second bell rather than explain to the owners why the wheel and the weather were introduced without him. It works, and the crews are quiet about it in the way of people who wanted a clause and got a crowbar. The mast, asked to note the method for the record, declines to creak.
effects: schedule 5 r18_ship_mutiny_reversal The mast has a petition and the storm has counsel

fail.text: The wedge holds until the boatswain's shoulder disagrees, and the door returns your proposal at speed with the full weight of ship's property behind it. You wake in the galley with a bandaged head and a formal caution. The storm, the cook reports, watched the whole thing and has amended its brief to note that some parties are unserious.
effects: schedule 5 r18_ship_mutiny_reversal The mast has a petition and the storm has counsel

choices.text: Refuse any bargain that unseats the chair

outcomes.text: The crews decline, politely and in writing, to trade away the only manager aboard who has never docked a wage. The refusal is unanimous, and unanimity at a steady heel is its own kind of weather. Captain Final Word adjourns to consult his authority, and his authority, lately, keeps consulting back.

outcomes.text: Holding the line means holding the meeting, and holding the meeting means chairing beside the mast through the wet half of the night while the captain waits you out. He adjourns at dawn, having conceded nothing except that you would not leave. You cough all morning, and the mast drips on you in what the cook rules is sympathy.

text:
SHIP MUTINY

Mid storm, Captain Final Word declares the weather severe at last, since only a third degree emergency lets him rule by decree without the reading's consent. The clerk goes pale in the lamplight and reads the next clause aloud anyway, professional to the bone: in weather of the third degree, command devolves upon the standing structure of the vessel. There is exactly one element of the standing structure with recognized management status, minuted, seconded, and pending. Every head on deck turns to the mainmast.

choices.text: Sell him a resignation letter dated yesterday

outcomes.text: You produce a ghostwritten resignation, dated the day before the declaration and drafted in a convincingly captainly hand, and you price the dignity in it by the syllable. He pays without haggling, which is the saddest thing you have ever been paid for. The clerk files it under carpentry, and the mast's in tray gains a copy, pinned by someone with a sense of history and no signature.
effects: schedule 2 r18_ship_mutiny_legacy The mast has a petition and the storm has counsel

choices.text: Work the mast's orders through the worst of it

success.text: The mast issues no commands, being a mast, but its standing orders are legible to anyone who looks up: reef what strains, turn from what howls, keep the people off the lee rail. The crews execute them like a shanty everyone already knows. The ship comes through ugly and floating, which in the articles and everywhere else is the definition of well commanded.
effects: schedule 2 r18_ship_mutiny_legacy The mast has a petition and the storm has counsel

fail.text: You are aloft executing policy when the storm queries your grip, and the deck receives you like an in tray receiving one more grievance. The cook counts your fingers twice and finds all of them, which she logs as the night's best outcome. The ship holds, the mast holds, and you hold what remains of your dignity in a sling.
effects: schedule 2 r18_ship_mutiny_legacy The mast has a petition and the storm has counsel

choices.text: Certify the succession before the storm adjourns

outcomes.text: You draw up the instrument of devolution on the driest table left, and the clerk countersigns without being asked, because his loyalty was never to the captain, it was to the index. Command of the vessel passes to the mainmast, care of the reading, effective the last clause. Captain Final Word swears by the articles one final time, accurately, at volume, into the wind.

outcomes.text: The certificate is perfect for the four seconds before the wind accepts it as an exhibit. You watch your best clause head for the horizon at attachment speed. The succession stands anyway, witnessed by the whole deck, but the paperwork is now in the storm's custody, and the storm does not return originals.
effects: schedule 2 r18_ship_mutiny_legacy The mast has a petition and the storm has counsel

text:
SHIP MUTINY

In harbor, under a rinsed sky, the ship rides at anchor while the crews argue destinations for the pure pleasure of being asked. The mainmast wears a brass plate, MIDDLE MANAGEMENT, ACTING SENIOR, polished every morning by the cook with the tenderness owed a good boss. Captain Final Word retains a cabin and a title, ceremonial signature, first class. The clerk now maintains the index for the reading, same pen, better clients.

choices.text: Help write the rota that picks captains and courses

success.text: The rota takes three drafts and one shouted evening, and what emerges is almost embarrassingly seaworthy: captains serve by turn and consent, courses win by vote of the whole mess, a term now defined, in writing, as everyone who eats. You are listed among the keepers of the book. The mast's plaque gains a small second line, chosen by the crews: CONSULTED ON ALL WEATHER.

fail.text: The destination debate reaches the question of a port with famous plums, and a thrown boot meant for the plums' loudest critic finds your ear instead. The rota gets finished by lamplight and ratified by everyone, including the boot's owner, who is assigned your laundry for a month by way of appeal. The vote, the cook observes, has excellent aim and poor manners.

choices.text: Give the mast a job description with teeth

outcomes.text: You draft the mast's duties tightly: convene readings, hold grievances, outrank captains in weather of the third degree, decline all other flattery. The clerk indexes it, the cook frames it, and the reading adopts it without amendment, a first. Somewhere below, the articles sit on the galley shelf between the salt and the honey, consulted daily, sworn by accurately.

The clerk indexes it, the cook frames it, and the reading adopts it without amendment, a first.
