You write it too well. Within a fortnight three other ships' masts have petitions nailed to them in three suspiciously familiar hands, and the harbormaster's office is drowning in structural grievances. You are invited ashore to explain the precedent, and the invitation has a docket number. The mast, informed it has started a fashion, creaks in a way the cook declines to translate.

choices.text: Sell the owners a report on preventing all this

outcomes.text: The shipping line's owners pay handsomely for your confidential study on preventing crew takeovers, and you write it thoroughly, elegantly, and minus the one clause that makes the whole thing work. They commission fleet wide reforms based on the redacted version. Every ship they own still has a mast, every mast still has eye level, and your fee clears before the first new petition goes up.

text:
ASTEROID MINE

Before breakfast. the vacuum miners' local discovers that Meteor Wealth Extraction has made oxygen deducted from miners' wages official policy. The dispute centers on the air locks. a spacesuit repossession agent has been appointed to keep the process dignified.

text:
MEMORY MARKET

Nine in the morning and a clerk from Nostalgia Capital is nailing a rate board to the fountain: first kisses four coins, snow days two, the smell of a grandmother's kitchen one and falling. Beside him a broker in a coat of many pockets takes bets that embarrassment will get cheaper, reasoning that once a fund owns your worst moment nobody can afford shame retail. From the vault doorway the archivist of unbought memories watches the queue form and asks whether you are busy this week.

choices.text: Help the archivist hide the vault index

success.text: You spend the morning moving the index out of plain sight, drawer by drawer, while the archivist explains the filing system: joy by smell, grief by weight, regret alphabetical because there is so much of it. The broker watches you carry the last drawer and touches his ear, which she says is his tell for respect. By noon you are on the vault rota, under a new sign facing the fountain: WE PAY NOTHING AND YOU KEEP EVERYTHING.
effects: schedule 4 r18_memory_market_assembly Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

fail.text: The clerk objects to the removal of inventory, the queue objects to the clerk, and in the resulting scrum you catch an elbow meant for the fountain. The archivist pulls you out by the collar and pronounces you hired, on the grounds that anyone who bleeds for an index deserves a drawer. The broker shorts your dignity on the spot and, insultingly, profits.
effects: schedule 4 r18_memory_market_assembly Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

choices.text: Report the rate board as an unlicensed exchange

outcomes.text: You observe to the clerk that the fountain is municipal, which makes his rate board an unlicensed financial exchange operating in a public bath. The System agrees with a speed suggesting it had been waiting for someone to ask, and the board comes down pending a permit, which requires a hearing, which requires a clerk who is on sabbatical indefinitely. The archivist files your argument under vinegar, her drawer for things that sharpen with age.
effects: schedule 4 r18_memory_market_assembly Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

outcomes.text: The clerk produces a permit, a countersigned permit, and a permit for producing permits, all in laminate. Worse, your objection is entered into the record as evidence of local market interest, which Nostalgia Capital cites by teatime in a circular about untapped demand. The rate for first kisses drops half a coin, and the circular thanks you by role if not by name.
effects: schedule 4 r18_memory_market_assembly Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

choices.text: Sell the summer you were nine

outcomes.text: The clerk pays eight coins for the pond, the dog, and the long light, and the broker pays six more for the embarrassing part with the pond, which he shorts before the ink dries, tipping his ear at you like a colleague. The archivist watches you count the coins and says nothing, which takes her a while. Walking home you can remember being happy that year and cannot remember why, which is a strange thing to learn about yourself at a discount.
effects: schedule 4 r18_memory_market_assembly Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

text:
MEMORY MARKET

Midnight in the recording vault, and the stainwright is brewing a pot of tea nobody will drink. The archivist has gathered the keepers of unbought memories around a single question: if Nostalgia Capital wants a golden age, why on earth sell them a real one. On the table sit blank diaries, a town map with a better summer sketched over it, and the first draft of the Marmalade Summer, a year so good that nobody remembers it yet.

choices.text: Punch up the golden age until it sells itself

outcomes.text: You give the Marmalade Summer a harvest so generous the granaries had to sleep in shifts, a regatta decided by a swan, and a bread recipe recorded in loving detail because auditors trust specifics. The bakers try the recipe for research purposes and it works, which is how the fake year starts improving the real one, loaves first. The stainwright ages your pages with the good tea and declares them plausibly ruined.
effects: schedule 5 r18_memory_market_retaliation Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

outcomes.text: Your draft is too good: three keepers weep over the swan and one old man insists he was actually there, which is the correct effect on the wrong audience. A visiting pot mender overhears the rehearsal and leaves town quickly, and by week's end Nostalgia Capital has heard a rumor it was always meant to hear, just sooner than planned. The stainwright doubles her tea order and stops sleeping.
effects: schedule 5 r18_memory_market_retaliation Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

choices.text: Sell the broker advance word of the vintage

outcomes.text: You find the broker at the dockside counting other people's regrets and tell him a golden age is about to surface, provenance immaculate. He pays eight for the tip and six for exclusivity, ear untouched, which means he either believes you or has stopped respecting you enough to lie. That night he builds a position in nostalgia futures the size of a modest cathedral, and whatever happens next, you are in it.
effects: schedule 5 r18_memory_market_retaliation Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

choices.text: Teach the town its new memories door to door

success.text: You go street by street with the list of things everyone remembers now, and the town takes to it like choir practice: the ferryman polishes his account of the swan, the bakers rehearse the smell. By Thursday two neighbors are arguing over whose grandmother invented the recipe, which is when you know the summer has taken. The archivist calls it the first honest lying this town has ever managed together.
effects: schedule 5 r18_memory_market_retaliation Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

fail.text: Most doors open to the idea, but one householder resents being told what she remembers and demonstrates, with a broom, what she remembers about trespassers. You retreat with a lump and her signature, since she liked the summer itself, just not the delivery. The stainwright logs your injury as production expenses, under tea.
effects: schedule 5 r18_memory_market_retaliation Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

text:
MEMORY MARKET

The appraiser arrives on the noon coach with a scale for weighing wonder and a licensed sniffer trained to tell old paper from paper that has merely been through eleven pots of tea. Nostalgia Capital does not buy stories, he announces at the vault door, it buys provenance. Behind him the broker lounges against the coach, openly shorting the appraiser's confidence, which everyone finds informative.

choices.text: Get the appraiser drunk and read his manifest

success.text: Four coins change hands, technically a consultancy fee for local color, and by the third bottle the appraiser is explaining his craft: the sniffer only ever checks the stains, never the ink. You also learn the firm resells a childhood at nine times cost once it is framed, which the appraiser calls the appreciation of appreciation. The keepers are cool with you for drinking with the enemy, but you walked out holding the lock and the key both.
effects: schedule 2 r18_memory_market_coalition Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

fail.text: The evening goes so well the appraiser retains you as resident authenticator, eight coins, ribbon included, effective immediately. This makes you professionally responsible for sniffing out forgeries you personally commissioned, a conflict of interest so pure it belongs in a museum. The town glares at the ribbon, the keepers glare at you, and the broker opens a book on how long you last.
effects: schedule 2 r18_memory_market_coalition Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

choices.text: Drill the whole town on the swan story

outcomes.text: By evening the town can recite the Marmalade Summer down to the weather, complete with the small contradictions the stainwright wrote in on purpose, since perfect agreement is what liars have. The bakers feed the rehearsal queue between takes, and the appraiser interviews forty residents and finds their accounts inconsistent in exactly the way truth is. He wires his firm two words, provenance holds, and the sniffer gets the afternoon off.
effects: schedule 2 r18_memory_market_coalition Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

outcomes.text: Rehearsal runs long on the ferry, where the swan reenactment capsizes into the shallows with you under the boom. You surface with a bruise and a decision: the regatta now ends in a famous capsize, which everyone agrees is easier to remember. The appraiser notes rising local excitement and revises his estimate upward, so it hurt, but it appraised well.
effects: schedule 2 r18_memory_market_coalition Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

choices.text: Demand a resident witness for every sniff

success.text: You cite the archive statutes: authentication of local memory requires a resident witness, and the town supplies one hundred and nine of them in rotation, each with opinions. Under that much supervision the sniffer checks four stains a day and the appraiser ages visibly, the only authentic aging on the premises. Nostalgia Capital, billed by the hour, begins urging its own man to hurry, which is exactly how you want a mark thinking.
effects: schedule 2 r18_memory_market_coalition Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

fail.text: The appraiser produces his license, which is signed by the System itself and annotated valid everywhere, always, in perpetuity, do not ask again. Your challenge is filed under seditious pedantry and the hearing fee is deducted from the town's patience. The queue of witnesses goes home, except the broker, who was never in it, just near it.
effects: schedule 2 r18_memory_market_coalition Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

text:
MEMORY MARKET

Three days before valuation, the stainwright runs out of the cheap black tea that dies convincingly on paper, and the entire golden age is held up on supplies. In the vault the keepers coach the ferryman through a regatta that never happened, while the broker, visiting daily now, offers odds on whether the town can hold a straight face. The archivist pins up a list titled THINGS WE ALL REMEMBER NOW and would like it learned by Thursday.

choices.text: Chip in for tea and run the dress rehearsal

outcomes.text: Three of your coins buy a crate of tea so cheap the merchant apologizes, which the stainwright pronounces perfect, since nothing ages like regret and budget leaves. The dress rehearsal runs from the fountain to the dock: the swan, the harvest, the argument about the grandmother, all in order. Afterward the bakers feed everyone from the fake recipe, and for an hour the golden age is simply happening, a little early.
effects: schedule 3 r18_memory_market_crisis Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

outcomes.text: The rehearsal collapses at the fountain when two families discover they have both been assigned the same heroic uncle. The dispute is settled with produce, some of which finds you. The archivist rules that the uncle was twins, everyone accepts this instantly, and you hold a cold cabbage to your eye while the golden age acquires its first apocrypha.
effects: schedule 3 r18_memory_market_crisis Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

choices.text: Stain every diary with a different year of tea

outcomes.text: You raid eleven pantries for eleven vintages of leaf, so each diary ages differently, the way real years do. When the sniffer eventually works the shelf it will find variety, and variety, the appraiser has explained to anyone who buys him a bottle, is what truth smells like. The stainwright studies your staggered stains and says, with professional envy, that you have given the lie a childhood of its own.
effects: schedule 3 r18_memory_market_crisis Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

outcomes.text: One borrowed tin turns out to be the archivist's heirloom leaf, worth more than the diary you soaked in it, and a visiting tea pedant announces that this vintage was embargoed the very year the diary claims. Nobody from the firm hears him, but the keepers now maintain a list of years it is safe to have existed in. The archivist forgives you at length, which is worse than not being forgiven.
effects: schedule 3 r18_memory_market_crisis Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

choices.text: Scalp early shares of the vintage to visitors

outcomes.text: You stand at the coach stop selling advance certificates of the Marmalade Summer to speculators who have heard something is appreciating here. Eight coins, then six more when a man in a good coat buys your last three at a premium, on the logic that anything selling this fast must be real. The broker watches your technique and dabs his eye, moved, he says, the way other men are moved by sunsets.
effects: schedule 3 r18_memory_market_crisis Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

text:
MEMORY MARKET

Valuation day, and the appraiser weighs the Marmalade Summer at the largest number his scale can print. Then the sniffer stops at one diary and sits down, the signal for genuine, and the room understands together: the stainwright bound a real memory into the fake year, the archivist's own, from the drawer that is never for sale. Nostalgia Capital smells appreciation and offers to buy the entire golden age, plus the vault it came in.

choices.text: Carve the vault out of the sale by statute

success.text: You produce the archive charter: the vault is a listed repository, its contents held in trust, and anything genuine found on the premises is an exhibit on loan, not stock. The firm's counsel searches the clause for a loophole, finds a second clause instead, and signs around the vault the way a river signs around a rock. The golden age sells, the building stays, and the archivist breathes for the first time since the sniffer sat.
effects: schedule 4 r18_memory_market_compromise Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

fail.text: The firm's revised draft arrives overnight with three words inserted at the definitions stage, and contents thereof, in the smallest font the law allows. You catch it on the second reading, after the toast but before the signing, which means the deal stalls in front of everyone with the pens already out. The appraiser looks personally betrayed, mostly because he chose the font size.
effects: schedule 4 r18_memory_market_compromise Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

choices.text: Countersign as local finder and take the fee

outcomes.text: The firm needs a resident signature to complete provenance and yours is available at the standard rate: eight coins on signing, six on completion. The broker watches you take firm money with a steady hand and touches his ear, and for a moment neither of you knows which one it is lying. That night you sleep fine, which you had not expected, and mention to no one.
effects: schedule 4 r18_memory_market_compromise Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

choices.text: Smuggle the real drawer out under their noses

outcomes.text: While the firm's men count zeros, the keepers run a relay of casseroles into the vault, dish by dish, and one dish leaves heavier than it arrived. The archivist's drawer spends the negotiation in the ferryman's pantry between the onions and the salt, which she later says is roughly where those years belonged anyway. The sniffer sniffs the empty slot, sits down again out of habit, and the appraiser records a second genuine, appraising, essentially, dust.

outcomes.text: You are halfway out the coal door with the drawer when it catches on the frame and springs open, and for one unprofessional moment the whole corridor smells of somebody's nineteenth of June. A firm man turns, you take the corner on your shoulder, and the bruise comes out the exact size of the drawer handle. The memory gets away unbought, which the archivist says is the only accounting she keeps.
effects: schedule 4 r18_memory_market_compromise Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

text:
MEMORY MARKET

Contract week, and the firm's dockside warehouse stands stacked with crated childhoods awaiting frames, every crate wearing a lot number where a name used to be. Nostalgia Capital's final terms arrive: the golden age, the vault building, and a licensing fee owed whenever a resident remembers privately. The broker reads the draft twice, goes pale in a new way, and shorts the firm's sincerity for everything he has.

choices.text: Crowbar the warehouse and send the lots home

success.text: You go in at low tide with a crowbar and a manifest, and by the third crate the night guards have found their own childhoods in the inventory, lots eighty one and eighty two, at which point they resign by walking away carrying them. Unguarded warehouses empty efficiently, and by dawn the crates are home and the frames are kindling. The keepers are furious at the method, the archivist says they were winning politely, but she says it while alphabetizing what you brought back.
effects: schedule 5 r18_memory_market_reversal Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

fail.text: The replacement guards are not local, not sentimental, and contractually immune to their own childhoods, which the firm keeps in a separate city as a precaution. You leave through a window you did not open on the way in, bringing one crate that proves to hold the firm's spare stationery. Three days in bed, and the stationery, the stainwright points out, is at least excellent for forging letterhead.
effects: schedule 5 r18_memory_market_reversal Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

choices.text: Hold a public remembering nobody can invoice

outcomes.text: The town answers the licensing clause by remembering out loud in the square all evening, at volume, in chorus, which under the firm's own definitions is publication, not private recollection, and owes nothing. Clerks circle the crowd trying to establish who is remembering what, but grief and recipes overlap in a manner resistant to invoicing. The firm's enforcement men stand down for lack of a billable unit, and the bakers feed them too, out of spite so warm it resembles hospitality.
effects: schedule 5 r18_memory_market_reversal Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

outcomes.text: The remembering runs hot, someone starts in on the firm's parentage, and a shoving match with the clerks knocks you into the fountain rail. The archivist bandages your head with the air of a woman who has read this contract closer than you have. Publicly, though, the town liked the taste of remembering at volume, and the clause is quietly unenforceable from tonight.
effects: schedule 5 r18_memory_market_reversal Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

choices.text: Sign, but let their prospectus define memory

outcomes.text: You accept the licensing fee in full, then define remembering exactly as the firm's prospectus does: memories are securities, and a security is only realized when traded, so private recollection is not remembering, and owes nobody anything. For symmetry you add a small buyback clause, any lot proved inauthentic must be repurchased at issue price by whoever authenticated it, and counsel waves it through, since their appraiser is never wrong. The broker reads the final draft, is quiet for a long moment, and goes off to make one more position considerably larger.
effects: schedule 5 r18_memory_market_reversal Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

outcomes.text: Your redraft is elegant, but counsel strikes the definitions section unread, on the principle that anything a local proposes is a trick. The buyback clause survives only because it flatters the appraiser, which was, on reflection, the load bearing vanity all along. Terms sign at midnight, nobody is happy, and the pressure in town rises like tea coming to the boil.
effects: schedule 5 r18_memory_market_reversal Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

text:
MEMORY MARKET

Publication morning, and the town confesses in print: the Marmalade Summer never happened, signed by every rememberer, with the tea receipts attached as exhibits. Under the buyback clause the keepers preserved through contract week, the appraiser who certified the vintage must repurchase the whole golden age at issue price, and the short positions built all season come due against a firm suddenly worth its actual contents. In the panic on the dock the broker runs a finger down the warehouse manifest and stops at lot four thousand and nine, which is him, aged six, marked down.

choices.text: Turn certificates into claim tickets

success.text: The vault opens as a claims office, and the firm's certificates, lovingly engraved, turn out to make excellent claim tickets once you cross out security and write person. All week you match lot numbers to faces, and each handover is the same small ceremony: the crate, the pause, the moment someone gets back a smell they had stopped being able to afford. The broker works the counter beside you without being asked, and when lot four thousand and nine surfaces he pays for it himself, full issue price plus interest, the worst trade of his career and the only one he does not hedge.
effects: schedule 2 r18_memory_market_legacy Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

fail.text: Word travels faster than the counter moves, and the morning crowd carries you into the doorframe hard enough to rattle the index. Order is restored by the archivist, who announces that memories go home oldest owners first, and the crowd sorts itself by grandmother with terrifying speed. You keep your post with a cold compress, and by dusk the crates are going home anyway, which is the kind of arithmetic that forgives a bruise.
effects: schedule 2 r18_memory_market_legacy Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

choices.text: File memory as an organ, not an asset

outcomes.text: You bring the System the paperwork of the collapse and a modest amendment: memory reclassified from asset to organ, on the precedent that nobody may securitize a spleen. The System, which respects filing above outcomes, stamps it in triplicate, and the entire market becomes retroactively anatomical. Nostalgia Capital's remaining inventory converts from portfolio to organ bank, a phrase whose paperwork burden no firm survives.
effects: schedule 2 r18_memory_market_legacy Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

outcomes.text: The firm appeals, and the System freezes all disputed memories in escrow, including, awkwardly, the Marmalade Summer, which now legally exists pending review. The town is ordered not to remember the fake year until a ruling, the one instruction guaranteed to keep it evergreen. Your filing fee is not refunded, and the escrow clerk asks you privately what the bread smelled like.
effects: schedule 2 r18_memory_market_legacy Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

choices.text: Cash your shorts while the firm is still falling

outcomes.text: Your positions, built quietly since the broker taught you the shape of a falling market, pay eight coins and then six more as the firm's paper finds its true weight, which is roughly firewood. On the side you pick up three distressed childhoods at panic prices, purely as an investment, you tell the archivist, who writes your name in a drawer you are not shown. The broker settles up with you at dusk and touches his ear, and this time you know exactly which of you it is.
effects: schedule 2 r18_memory_market_legacy Nostalgia Capital pays cash for childhoods. Ask why.

text:
MEMORY MARKET

A month on, the vault keeps market hours: withdrawals free, deposits voluntary, valuations refused on principle. The old rate board hangs inside as a warning, repainted with one line, EVERYTHING HERE APPRECIATES AND NOTHING HERE IS FOR SALE. At the counter the broker counts out the last installment on lot four thousand and nine, buying at the top of the only market he ever misjudged, and looking, for a ruined man, remarkably solvent.

choices.text: Catalog the whole con as the vault's exhibit

outcomes.text: You file the entire caper, rate board to receipts to collapse, as one continuous memory and shelve it under acquisitions, at cost. Visiting scouts from two other firms read the exhibit to the end, take careful notes at the buyback clause, and are last seen pursuing quieter careers. The archivist stains the final page herself, one ring from the good tea, and calls the exhibit finished, provenance impeccable, since for once every word of it happened.

Visiting scouts from two other firms read the exhibit to the end, take careful notes at the buyback clause, and are last seen pursuing quieter careers. The archivist stains the final page herself, one ring from the good tea, and calls the exhibit finished, provenance impeccable, since for once every word of it happened.

A traveling auditor rules that the exhibit, being valued by visitors, constitutes a security, and demands it be assessed. The archivist assesses it at one story, payable in kind, and the auditor leaves owing the vault an anecdote he will not miss until later. The paperwork of his objection joins the exhibit, which the stainwright says is the closest that man will come to being remembered.

choices.text: Sell your memoir of the caper to a rival firm

outcomes.text: A rival firm pays eight coins for your account of the method and six more for the appendix on tea. You leave out the load bearing part, which is a town willing to lie in chorus, so their scouts depart confident, carrying a recipe with no oven, toward some other town's fountain. The broker reads your memoir at the counter and says it is accurate everywhere it does not matter, which he means as a review and you keep as a blurb.

choices.text: Take the empty drawer and the keeper's oath

success.text: The archivist gives you a drawer of your own and the oath, which is one sentence long and requires no signature, the vault having had its fill of instruments. The broker takes the post of house authenticator, on the reasoning that nobody spots a counterfeit like a man who bought one back at a loss, and his ear, at rest at last, becomes the vault's most reliable instrument. Your first deposit is the whole long season just ended, filed under vinegar with the archivist's blessing, already appreciating.

fail.text: The oath goes fine, the furniture does not: moving lot four thousand and nine into permanent storage, you set the crate down on your own foot, and the broker laughs so hard he has to sit on it. You limp to the counter and receive your drawer anyway, along with the vault's standing advice, lift with your knees, remember with everything else. It is, the archivist notes, the correct sort of injury: entirely yours, and appreciating.

text:
FUNERAL INSURANCE

Tuesday morning, the widow with the receipt tin lays a denial letter on the gravedigger's table like a fish that has gone off. Her husband Mott is three days dead and LastChance Assurance has declined the claim: mortality, the letter explains, was a preexisting condition, present at inception. It was hand delivered by their actuary, a neat man who has priced death for forty years and appears to have just now met one. The bereaved families' circle wants exactly one payout for exactly one funeral, and the actuary keeps asking whether the deceased is available to comment.

choices.text: Demand the clause that ruled being alive fatal

outcomes.text: The branch clerk, cornered, produces Exclusion 14(b): loss arising from conditions present at birth. You ask, politely, which of LastChance's customers was not born, and request the list of eligible policyholders. The clerk starts writing names, stops, and stamps the claim PENDING REVIEW, which in their dialect is a scream. The actuary copies the whole exchange into his notebook under the heading anomalies.
effects: schedule 5 r18_funeral_insurance_assembly LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

outcomes.text: The branch clerk agrees the clause is absurd, then explains that absurdity is covered under a separate policy the widow does not hold. Your appeal goes into the drawer marked SEASONAL, which is opened once a year to see if anyone has given up. Word gets round the lane that the law read the letter and blinked first.
effects: schedule 5 r18_funeral_insurance_assembly LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

choices.text: Sign LastChance's form ruling the death voluntary

outcomes.text: LastChance pays fourteen for your signature on a form stating that the deceased engaged in high risk behavior, namely continuing to breathe past sixty. The coin is real and the ink is fast. The widow says nothing, which somehow itemizes. The actuary shakes your hand and remarks that you are his first purchase of a conscience at market rate.
effects: schedule 5 r18_funeral_insurance_assembly LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

choices.text: Put three coins in the tin and take a rota shift

outcomes.text: You put three coins in the receipt tin and the widow does not thank you, she promotes you: Thursdays you carry, Sundays you cook. The gravedigger opens a page in his ledger titled GRAVES OWED TO US and writes your name in the paying column. It is the first ledger you have ever been glad to appear in. The actuary asks what rate of return you expect and is quietly devastated by the answer.

outcomes.text: You help carry Mott down to the gravedigger's cold cellar to wait out the paperwork, and the cellar stairs win the argument with your spine. Upstairs the neighbors are already shouting about the letter, and the shouting has begun to draft demands. The widow tucks a blanket over you and puts your name in the tin anyway, filed under good for it.

text:
FUNERAL INSURANCE

Wednesday night the circle convenes in the gravedigger's kitchen, eleven mourners, one kettle, and the policy itself in the middle of the table wearing its own weight. Nobody has ever read it entire: it runs to forty pages and it grew three riders on the walk over. The actuary attends in a corner, notebook out, having requested permission to observe a loss event in its natural habitat. The kettle has been to more funerals than he has.

choices.text: Sell top-up grief cover to the mourners at the door

outcomes.text: You take a commission from LastChance to offer the mourners Supplementary Bereavement Cover, which insures against the failure of the primary policy and is underwritten by the same people who failed it. Fourteen coins by nine o'clock, because grief buys anything wearing a stamp. The actuary logs your sales technique under predation, observed. The kettle declines to pour for you, and a kettle's opinion follows a man around a small town.
effects: schedule 2 r18_funeral_insurance_retaliation LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

choices.text: Pass the hat before anyone reads a word

outcomes.text: The hat goes around before the policy gets a hearing, because burying Mott should not have to wait on comprehension. It comes back heavy: coins, a brooch, one pledge of two pigs written on a hymn sheet. The actuary examines the hat, cannot locate the interest rate, and asks with real fear what secures the instrument. The widow says grief does, and he writes it down with a steady hand and a shaken face.

outcomes.text: The hat goes round but so does the letter, and the room catches fire in the argumentative sense. In the crush to be angriest, an elbow finds your eye. Still, the tin ends the night heavier, and the widow holds it like a verdict. Outside, somebody is teaching the street to say preexisting condition in a tone the street will remember.
effects: schedule 2 r18_funeral_insurance_retaliation LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

choices.text: Read the whole policy aloud without stopping

success.text: You read all forty pages aloud, and the room learns that clause nine excludes death by weather, clause twenty two excludes death by absence of weather, and clause thirty one defines the word beneficiary to mean LastChance. By page thirty the policy is visibly sweating. Documents are like this: read entire, in company, they lose the power to surprise, which was the only power they had. The actuary checks his own copy against your reading, finds the two do not match, and sits very still, like a man discovering his church keeps two bibles.
effects: schedule 2 r18_funeral_insurance_retaliation LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

fail.text: You read aloud as far as clause forty four, wherein the policy defines reading aloud as a claims event, subject to excess. The room boggles, then despairs, then puts the kettle on again. LastChance will later cite tonight as proof the document was handled without gloves. The actuary, to his own surprise, stays to help wash up.
effects: schedule 2 r18_funeral_insurance_retaliation LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

text:
FUNERAL INSURANCE

Friday's post brings eleven identical envelopes: LastChance has repriced every mourner in the circle under a new Survivor Loading, grief being, actuarially speaking, contagious. The widow's premium now exceeds her rent, which at least gives the rent something to aspire to. Down in the cold cellar Mott waits with the patience the dead are famous for. The actuary delivered the envelopes personally and then stood a long time in the lane, doing arithmetic on his cuff and disliking every answer.

choices.text: Pool the loadings so nobody lapses alone

outcomes.text: The circle pays the loadings out of one pot, which annoys LastChance twice over: they wanted eleven quiet lapses, and instead they have accidentally invented one large customer with a grudge. The gravedigger's ledger sprouts a premiums column, next to graves, under the general heading THINGS WE OWE EACH OTHER. The actuary reviews the pot's mathematics and pronounces it unsound, insupportable, and working.
effects: schedule 3 r18_funeral_insurance_coalition LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

outcomes.text: The pot covers everyone except the last envelope, and you make up the shortfall by selling your good coat in a cold week, which your chest records as an outstanding balance. Two doors down, somebody burns their notice in the lane and the smoke smells like a mood. The widow rules the coat a loan against the fund, and the fund keeps better books than LastChance.
effects: schedule 3 r18_funeral_insurance_coalition LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

choices.text: File a grievance against the Survivor Loading

outcomes.text: You file at the System registry, where a bored adjudicator reads Survivor Loading twice and invites LastChance to produce one case of grief proving fatal within the policy term. Their counsel cites Mott's widow, on the grounds that she may yet die of it, and the adjudicator suspends the loading on the spot. The tin gets a fortnight's peace. The actuary is named in the filing as the loading's author and requests, in writing, that his name be spelled wrong.
effects: schedule 3 r18_funeral_insurance_coalition LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

outcomes.text: The registry accepts your grievance, weighs it, and rules the loading lawful because grief was not disclosed at inception. You point out that at inception nobody had died yet, and the clerk marks the argument speculative. The loading stands, the tin thins, and the lane coins a name for the registry that appears on none of its forms.
effects: schedule 3 r18_funeral_insurance_coalition LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

choices.text: Impound the courier's crate of denial letters

success.text: You and the gravedigger relieve the courier of a crate holding sixty denial letters and store it in the cold cellar, where bad news keeps. Sixty households get one more week of believing they are insured, which is not nothing, and LastChance must reissue the lot at its own postage. The circle disapproves of your methods at a volume that suggests admiration. The actuary reports the crate as lost in transit, misspelling his own name as previously requested.
effects: schedule 3 r18_funeral_insurance_coalition LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

fail.text: The courier turns out to be an enforcement adjunct with a truncheon rated for exactly this. You wake up in the lane considerably reduced, and the crate is long gone. By evening the branch has a guard on the door whose salary alone disproves their poverty at claims time. The gravedigger carries you home and does not say told you so, out loud.
effects: schedule 3 r18_funeral_insurance_coalition LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

text:
FUNERAL INSURANCE

Saturday market fills with strangers holding familiar envelopes: bereaved from three parishes over, each denied for a different reason, each reason contradicting the others. One death was excluded as an act of god, one because appendix D defines god as a competing underwriter, one for occurring outdoors. Laid side by side on the fish stall, the letters begin, very quietly, to disagree. The actuary walks the rows of paper like a man at an exhibition of his own handwriting.

choices.text: Bind the denials into one self-refuting volume

outcomes.text: You bind forty denial letters into a single volume in which LastChance rules death impossible indoors, compulsory outdoors, and in one memorable case a breach of the deceased's warranty of continued existence. Read as one book, the exclusions cancel out like debts between liars. You lodge a copy at the registry and one at the branch, addressed to whom it may concern, which is everyone. The actuary borrows it overnight and returns it annotated in a hand that trembles by the appendix.
effects: schedule 4 r18_funeral_insurance_crisis LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

effects: schedule 4 r18_funeral_insurance_crisis LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

outcomes.text: Your bound volume is admitted by the registry as evidence, then immediately reclassified as a competing insurance product and taxed accordingly. The stamp duty eats the tin's margin for the month. LastChance sends round a note welcoming you to the industry, with rates.
effects: schedule 4 r18_funeral_insurance_crisis LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

choices.text: Broker the letters to a rival underwriter

outcomes.text: A rival house pays fourteen for the stack: raw data on what LastChance customers will swallow, priced by parish. Their new product undercuts LastChance by denying claims twenty percent faster. The strangers get their letters back eventually, creased by other men's thumbs. The actuary reads the rival prospectus and remarks that you have introduced natural selection to a species that should not be encouraged to breed.
effects: schedule 4 r18_funeral_insurance_crisis LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

choices.text: Queue with the strangers while they file

success.text: Forty bereaved file forty claims in one afternoon, and you hold places in the queue for the ones whose knees have opinions. The branch's PENDING stamp runs dry by four o'clock, which the clerk reports upward as an act of god, which appendix D makes awkward. Three parishes now know the widow's kitchen by smell. The actuary tallies the queue and discovers, with visible horror, that the customers have found out about each other.
effects: schedule 4 r18_funeral_insurance_crisis LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

fail.text: The queue holds until the branch declares an early closure for stocktaking, the stock being excuses. In the crush at the door a hinge takes the skin off your knuckles. The strangers leave uncounted but not unconvinced, and the widow's kitchen gains three parishes of correspondents. LastChance posts a notice limiting grief to appointment only.
effects: schedule 4 r18_funeral_insurance_crisis LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

text:
FUNERAL INSURANCE

Monday, the System convenes a public hearing, and LastChance's counsel announces the doctrine aloud: mortality attaches at birth, therefore every death is a preexisting condition, therefore the product insures against an event that cannot occur to anyone eligible to buy it. The gallery, mostly widows, does the arithmetic in silence. The actuary is called as the doctrine's expert witness and takes the stand holding his own mortality table like a confession. In the cold cellar, Mott enters his third week of administrative delay.

choices.text: Pocket the settlement and develop no memory

outcomes.text: Counsel finds you at the recess with fourteen coins and a confidentiality rider, payable on condition that you develop no memory of the doctrine. You sign, the coins are real, and silence turns out to be a renewable. In the gallery the widow moves along one seat, which in widow is a paragraph. The actuary watches the transaction and enters it in his table under losses, uninsured.
effects: schedule 5 r18_funeral_insurance_compromise LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

outcomes.text: Counsel finds you at the recess with fourteen coins and a confidentiality rider, payable on condition that you develop no memory of the doctrine. The actuary watches the transaction and enters it in his table under losses, uninsured.
effects: schedule 5 r18_funeral_insurance_compromise LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

choices.text: Stand the gallery up, one exclusion at a time

success.text: You ask the bereaved in the gallery to stand as their exclusions are read out, and by clause nine the room is upright and counsel is reciting the doctrine to a forest. Nobody shouts, which is worse for LastChance than shouting. The adjudicator adjourns to consider whether a product can lawfully exist that no living person can claim under. The actuary, still sworn in, rises with them, technically in error, actually in attendance.
effects: schedule 5 r18_funeral_insurance_compromise LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

fail.text: The gallery stands, and the bailiffs read standing as unrest, which is at least the day's first accurate classification. You take a bailiff's elbow keeping the widow's feet clear of the aisle. The hearing adjourns in disorder with the doctrine intact, but three more parishes hear about the room that stood up. The actuary's table goes home dogeared at the page for heart.
effects: schedule 5 r18_funeral_insurance_compromise LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

choices.text: Cross-examine the actuary about his zero

outcomes.text: You ask the actuary one question: in forty years of his tables, how many claims has LastChance paid in full. He consults his records for a long time, though the number does not require consulting, being round. He says it into the record, and the zero lands in the hearing room like a coffin nobody ordered. The adjudicator orders LastChance's books opened, and counsel objects on the grounds that the books are load bearing.
effects: schedule 5 r18_funeral_insurance_compromise LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

outcomes.text: You ask the actuary whether death can preexist itself, and counsel objects that philosophy is excluded under appendix F. The adjudicator, who is salaried by the hour, sustains. The doctrine survives the day, but the actuary's answer, begun and abandoned, is entered in the record as a hesitation of unusual length. Hesitations that long have ended engagements in quieter rooms.
effects: schedule 5 r18_funeral_insurance_compromise LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

text:
FUNERAL INSURANCE

Thursday, LastChance offers terms: a Grief Rider paying half the funeral, provided the family certifies that Mott was never technically alive, no life having been disclosed at inception. Half a funeral is defined in the schedule as one procession, no hymns, grave rented. The widow reads it twice and asks which half of her husband it covers. The actuary, who drafted neither the rider nor the joke, has begun spending his evenings at the kitchen table, doing sums for the tin and eating like an apology.

choices.text: Refuse the half funeral and fund a whole one

outcomes.text: The circle declines to certify Mott out of existence and resolves to fund the whole funeral itself: coffin from the joiner on promise, hymns from the choir on faith, grave from the gravedigger at the ancient rate of one favor. The tin cannot cover it and covers it anyway, which is the tin's entire doctrine. The actuary reruns the numbers three times, announces that the fund is impossible, and asks to keep helping.
effects: schedule 2 r18_funeral_insurance_reversal LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

effects: schedule 2 r18_funeral_insurance_reversal LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

outcomes.text: The refusal carries, but the joiner's credit does not, and you spend the night hauling seasoned oak across town on a borrowed barrow with an unseasoned wheel. Your shoulder files a grievance of its own. By morning there is a coffin in the kitchen and a rumor in the lane that this circle buries its own regardless, which is the sort of rumor that pays interest.
effects: schedule 2 r18_funeral_insurance_reversal LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

choices.text: Amend their rider to invoice forty years of premiums

success.text: You return the rider amended: if Mott was never alive, then LastChance owes forty years of premiums collected on a nonexistent risk, plus interest, plus the widow's time. Counsel reads it, goes pale in the way of a man doing actual arithmetic, and withdraws the Grief Rider before it can testify. Withdrawn documents lose seniority, and the other exclusions begin edging away from it in the file. The actuary confirms your interest figure to the farthing, unprompted, in front of witnesses.
effects: schedule 2 r18_funeral_insurance_reversal LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

fail.text: Your amendment is ruled a counteroffer, which voids their offer, which counsel celebrates as a settlement failure attributable to the claimant. The half funeral leaves the table and takes the table's goodwill with it. The widow observes that she has been robbed politer. You keep the draft: it is the first document in this whole affair that anyone was afraid of.
effects: schedule 2 r18_funeral_insurance_reversal LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

choices.text: Take LastChance's coin to index the archive cellar

success.text: LastChance pays four a day for somebody to index their archive cellar, and you take the job to the circle's audible disgust. Under sixty years of shed exclusions you find the founding stock: first edition policies, one page each, and a premium ledger in which one small account has never once lapsed. You copy that account number onto your wrist and index everything else exactly wrong. The actuary signs your day sheet without looking up, which from him is a handshake.
effects: schedule 2 r18_funeral_insurance_reversal LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

fail.text: You take the indexing coin, then a speculator's coin on top for the cellar's inventory list, and the lane hears about both before supper. The speculator's men visit the archive by night and leave with a wagonload of history priced by weight. Whatever was down there is an asset abroad now. The kitchen still feeds you, but your bowl arrives without comment, which is how a kitchen forecloses.
effects: schedule 2 r18_funeral_insurance_reversal LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

text:
FUNERAL INSURANCE

Sunday, sorting the receipt tin for the premium ledger, the widow finds, folded under sixty years of tuppenny stamps, a first edition LastChance policy in the name of Mott's grandmother, transferable to issue, never lapsed. It is one page. It says: upon any death whatsoever, howsoever, whensoever, this house pays, no condition being prior to the human condition. The actuary reads it standing up, then sits down the way men do at news. It predates every exclusion LastChance has ever bred, and by their own doctrine it attached at birth, to everyone the grandmother ever mourned for.

choices.text: Serve the grandmother's page as controlling law

outcomes.text: The registry rules in a single afternoon: the first edition was never repealed, only buried, and documents, unlike people, are covered against that. LastChance owes the funeral, the arrears, and under its own doctrine of attachment at birth, sixty years of interest, payable in coin or, by an old schedule nobody ever amended, in provisions. The circle takes provisions, and the wake is now catered into next spring. Counsel asks for time to consult the appendices and is informed that the appendices are junior to the page.
effects: schedule 3 r18_funeral_insurance_legacy LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

effects: schedule 3 r18_funeral_insurance_legacy LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

outcomes.text: The registry accepts the page, then loses it for two days inside its own intake process, during which LastChance files sixteen motions defining grandmother. The page resurfaces with a queue number and a coffee ring. The claim survives, delayed, and the lane has stopped taking delay quietly. The actuary swears an affidavit that the coffee ring is not his and, unprompted, that the page is genuine.
effects: schedule 3 r18_funeral_insurance_legacy LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

choices.text: Sell the original page to LastChance's museum

outcomes.text: LastChance offers fourteen for the original, for their museum, they say, and their museum hums like a shredder. You broker the sale: the widow's tin is lighter one heirloom and the town lighter one precedent. The settlement coin goes to the grain merchant, who, hearing where it came from, raises his prices to meet the demand he has just invented. The actuary appraises the page on its way out and quietly copies it word for word, which you will not learn about for some time.
effects: schedule 3 r18_funeral_insurance_legacy LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

choices.text: Register the whole lane under the tuppenny policy

outcomes.text: The page covers the holder and any household she mourned for, and Mott's grandmother, by the testimony of everyone old enough, mourned for the entire lane on principle. You pay the three coin transfer stamp out of your own pocket and register the lot: forty households attach to one tuppenny policy like swallows to an eave. LastChance's ledger now shows its oldest product as its largest. The actuary certifies each attachment in his own hand, and his signature gets steadier as the list gets longer.
effects: schedule 3 r18_funeral_insurance_legacy LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

outcomes.text: Registration day draws a queue, the queue draws a LastChance inspector, and the inspector's clipboard draws a crowd with opinions. You get between the clipboard and the widow and collect a bruise with excellent provenance. Half the lane attaches before the branch shutters drop. The other half writes its names in the gravedigger's ledger instead, under NEXT.
effects: schedule 3 r18_funeral_insurance_legacy LastChance rules death preexisting. The lane objects.

text:
FUNERAL INSURANCE

On a clear Thursday Mott is buried at last, paid in full, hymns included, the invoice marked settled in a hand that shook slightly. The whole lane walks behind the coffin, and behind the lane walks the actuary, attending, at seventy, his first funeral. He has priced this exact morning forty thousand times. He stands at the grave doing no arithmetic at all, which for him is prayer, and afterward he fills out a claim form naming himself: one actuary, total loss, cause, attendance at an uninsured event.

choices.text: Accept the vacant post as head of exclusions

outcomes.text: LastChance, suddenly short one actuary, offers you his office, his inkwell, and fourteen on signature, exclusions being a growth business in every climate. You take it, reasoning that somebody was going to, and you at least know where the bodies are itemized. The circle strikes your name from the tin without ceremony, ceremony being reserved these days for people they can afford to lose. Your first directive arrives preprinted: reinstate the loadings.

The circle strikes your name from the tin without ceremony, ceremony being reserved these days for people they can afford to lose. Your first directive arrives preprinted: reinstate the loadings.

choices.text: Keep the books for the lane's burial fund

outcomes.text: The tin is promoted to a strongbox and the strongbox to a doctrine: tuppence a week, any death whatsoever, no exclusions, and the only appendix is the casserole rota. You keep the ledger with the gravedigger, and the retired actuary audits it monthly for the fee of one dinner, eaten slowly. His forty years of mortality tables, pointed the other way round, price tuppence a week to the farthing and leave room for hymns. His old claim form hangs above the strongbox, stamped PAID by the widow, who says every fund should frame its first loss.

The fund opens on a wet Sunday and the whole parish arrives at once, which the kitchen was never underwritten for. Carrying the second bench in, you find the doorframe unwilling to negotiate. By dark there are three hundred names down at tuppence a week and one lump on your head, which the retired actuary insists on recording as the fund's first claim, settled in cake.

choices.text: Deliver the eulogy as a closing audit

success.text: You eulogize Mott by the numbers: married forty one years, owed nothing, owed everything, premiums paid in full at every counter that ever had him queue. The lane laughs where laughing was filed and cries where crying was provisioned. At the end the retired actuary asks for your page, folds it into his coat over the heart, and marks it paid. LastChance sends a wreath with an invoice attached, and the gravedigger plants the invoice, curious what such a thing grows.

fail.text: Your audit eulogy runs long, and grief keeps interrupting the arithmetic, which is proper but hard on the delivery. A LastChance observer at the gate notes the crowd size for future loadings. The lane walks home in the rain arguing pleasantly with your figures. The retired actuary tells you the numbers were wrong and the accounting was right, and buys you a drink his pension can barely carry.

text:

text:
PUBLIC LIBRARY

Tuesday, nine sharp, and a man in a soft grey suit is explaining that the public stacks are now Knowledge Access Premium, four coppers per chapter, rewinding extra. During the handover audit the deputy head of Returns finds an index card in drawer forty: one volume out, An Honest Map of Who Owned What, due back eight centuries ago, fee compounding daily. The account manager goes very still and says that particular title is deaccessioned, closed, and not to be pursued. The deputy head of Returns has never once in her career heard the words not to be pursued.

choices.text: Pay three coppers toward the fee to reopen the file

outcomes.text: Three coppers against a debt with more digits than the kingdom has clerks, and the ledger accepts them, because ledgers cannot refuse a payment. The account is now active, which means the book is now expected, which means somebody, somewhere, is late. The deputy head of Returns stamps your receipt twice, once for the record and once, you suspect, for love. The librarians' defense brigade signs you in on the after-hours sheet, in pencil, which is how they say welcome.
effects: schedule 3 r18_public_library_assembly The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

outcomes.text: Three coppers in, and drawer forty, unopened since the interest tables were last recalculated by hand, comes off its runners and delivers eight centuries of accrued paperwork onto your foot. The deputy head of Returns bandages you with archival tape, which she notes is acid free and therefore kinder than ordinary sympathy. Word gets around the reading room that the fee has drawn blood already, and the room does not take it quietly.
effects: schedule 3 r18_public_library_assembly The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

choices.text: File the fee as an active public debt

outcomes.text: You register the fee with the registry as an outstanding receivable of the public, which makes the book legally in circulation, which makes deaccessioned a word the account manager can no longer afford. He asks to see the relevant statute, and the deputy head of Returns hands him the lending code, all nine volumes, reference only, no removals. Somewhere inside the soft grey suit, a schedule dies quietly.
effects: schedule 3 r18_public_library_assembly The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

outcomes.text: The registry clerk finds a subclause stating that debts older than the registry are the registry's to forgive, and that forgiveness is available at premium rates. Knowledge Access Premium files for the forgiveness concession before lunch. You have taught the machine where the wound is, and the machine is grateful in the way machines are.
effects: schedule 3 r18_public_library_assembly The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

choices.text: Misfile the card where no audit will look

outcomes.text: The account manager pays in two envelopes, one for the service and one for the professionalism, and you slide the ancient fee into Fiction under Maritime Romance, where no auditor has ever survived. It works. The stacks convert to timed rentals by Friday and the turnstiles sing their little coin song. The deputy head of Returns refiles Fiction alphabetically every Sunday night, but nobody tells you that, and nothing in either envelope covers it.
effects: schedule 3 r18_public_library_assembly The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

text:
PUBLIC LIBRARY

Midnight in the map room, where the librarians' defense brigade has built an evidence wall out of catalog drawers and red thread rated for archival use. The archivist emeritus, who retired forty years ago and simply never mentioned it, has dated the fee by its ink: nine days older than the kingdom. Nine days, she repeats, older than the kingdom that invented the fine. The borrower's signature is a smudge, and the smudge has been assigned its own acid-free sleeve pending interrogation.

choices.text: Raise the signature with lamp smoke and patience

success.text: Under the lamp the smudge resolves into half a name, written by somebody trained, expensive, and in a tremendous hurry. The archivist emeritus certifies your technique as reversible, the only compliment she owns. Half a name is not evidence, she says, but it is a call number, and the evidence wall gains a second thread.
effects: schedule 4 r18_public_library_retaliation The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

fail.text: The lamp flares, the sleeve buckles, and the smudge becomes a slightly larger, better documented smudge. The archivist emeritus enters your attempt in the log as enthusiastic, the worst word she knows. By morning the account manager is telling anyone with a newsletter that the library has taken to burning its own records.
effects: schedule 4 r18_public_library_retaliation The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

choices.text: Sell a facsimile of the card to a private collector

outcomes.text: A collector of provenance problems pays handsomely for a certified facsimile, no questions asked, which is his way of asking all of them. The money is real and folds beautifully. Within a week facsimiles of your facsimile are trading in three cities, and Knowledge Access Premium quietly buys the lot, which tells the brigade something you were paid not to notice.
effects: schedule 4 r18_public_library_retaliation The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

choices.text: Divide the watch: pages, bindery, bookmobile

outcomes.text: The pages take dawn, the bindery takes dusk, and the bookmobile driver, who fears no road, takes the hours with no name. Every shift signs the sheet and initials the margins, which is how a schedule gets knitted out of favors. The night stamper is issued the master date stamp and holds it the way other kingdoms hold a sword.

outcomes.text: The rota holds for two nights, and on the third you personally cover the hours with no name and meet the stepladder the pages forgot to fold. The archivist emeritus logs your injury as shelving incident, routine, because in her files nothing about this case is allowed to look special. The reading room hears the thump and draws its own conclusions, loudly.

text:
PUBLIC LIBRARY

Thursday the recall notices arrive, one per catalog drawer, signed by Knowledge Access Premium's Department of Retrospective Collection. Two contractors in matching aprons wait at the door with a handcart, a locksmith, and a work order for the entire card catalog, described as legacy media, hazardous. The deputy head of Returns reads the order twice and observes that it lacks a date stamp, which in this building is roughly a death certificate lacking a death.

choices.text: Barricade the catalog with the returns carts

success.text: The carts lock wheel to wheel with the ease of furniture that has waited its whole life for this, and the contractors discover that a loaded returns cart outweighs a work order. They withdraw to seek an escalation, which is contractor for goodbye. The night stamper stamps the abandoned handcart RECEIVED, a formality nobody asked for and everybody needed.
effects: schedule 5 r18_public_library_coalition The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

fail.text: The lead contractor has moved pianos and knows the shoulder trick, and you learn it too, briefly, in the ribs. They take a sample drawer, the wrong one, since the ancient fee sleeps in the map room now, but a drawer is a drawer and the loss register gains its first entry in forty years. The deputy head of Returns records it in her smallest handwriting, the size she saves for grief.
effects: schedule 5 r18_public_library_coalition The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

choices.text: Deal the catalog out to patrons, card by card

outcomes.text: By closing time the catalog has been checked out, one card per patron, each with a due date and a solemn look. The chain of custody now runs through four hundred coat pockets, and a chain like that cannot be handcarted. A patron who reads obituaries for sport takes the ancient fee's neighbor and promises it a shelf of its own.
effects: schedule 5 r18_public_library_coalition The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

The driver patches you up with the first aid kit and a lecture about scheduled stops. Four hundred cards ride home in four hundred pockets, and one of your fingers will be filing under a splint for a while.

Their counsel requests an extension, and the deputy head of Returns explains, gently, that extensions are for members in good standing.

text:
PUBLIC LIBRARY

Saturday's summit convenes in the bindery: two deed clerks, an ink grinder, a retired courier who once delivered for the crown, and the night stamper, who has brought the ancient fee and a magnifying lens on a ribbon. The date on the fee, he announces, was struck by a die the royal mint claims never existed, and he has the mint's letter claiming it, filed, cross-referenced. A room full of specialists leans in. The account manager has sent pastries, and a note that says nothing, twice.

choices.text: Pool every archive's fragments into one timeline

success.text: Deed clerks, courier, ink grinder: each holds one bead of the story, and by lamplight they string it. Nine days before the founding, one book left this building in official hands, and every record of those hands was afterward misplaced, officially, all at once. The timeline goes up on the evidence wall under glass, and everyone present initials the corner, which is the closest this room gets to a war cry.
effects: schedule 2 r18_public_library_crisis The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

fail.text: The timeline assembles beautifully right up until the courier and the ink grinder disagree about a Tuesday eight centuries gone, and specialist disagreement is a weather system. In the scuffle over the good lens you catch an elbow from a man who has been retired since before you were born. The pastries are declared compromised and the meeting adjourns loudly enough to reach the street.
effects: schedule 2 r18_public_library_crisis The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

choices.text: Ask the mint to deny the stamp die in writing

outcomes.text: You request, in quadruplicate, that the mint certify the nonexistence of die number one, and the mint, constitutionally unable to resist certifying, does. The certificate lists the die's dimensions, its alloy, and its storage location, properties nonexistent objects rarely enjoy. The deputy head of Returns files it under Admissions, Accidental, a folder that turns out to be thick.
effects: schedule 2 r18_public_library_crisis The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

choices.text: Auction seats at the evidence wall to the curious

outcomes.text: Provenance scandal is the season's fashionable anxiety, and the wealthy pay well to stand near documents while looking grave. You sell viewings by the quarter hour, souvenir thread included. The investigation funds itself now, which everyone agrees is convenient, in the tone people use when convenient is doing the work of a longer sentence. Knowledge Access Premium books three viewings under names that fool nobody.

text:
PUBLIC LIBRARY

Dawn finds the map room lock intact, the window latched, and the ancient fee gone from under its glass. Nothing else is touched except the after-hours sheet, where someone has signed the card out, time noted, in a confident hand nobody recognizes. The deputy head of Returns stares at the signature a long while and says only that whoever they are, they followed procedure, and she does not know whether to hunt them or hire them. By noon Knowledge Access Premium announces that the so-called ancient account has been settled privately and closed.

choices.text: Demand the settled account's paperwork, all of it

outcomes.text: Settled accounts generate paper the way wounds generate scabs, and you demand the full dressing: receipt, instrument, authorizing signature. Knowledge Access Premium produces a certificate of settlement signed by an officer who, the registry confirms, has been dead for a tidy while. A settlement signed by the deceased reopens the account automatically, per a rule the deputy head of Returns recites from memory, beaming.

outcomes.text: The account manager pays twice your asking price, which tells you the sheet frightened somebody upstairs. The unknown signature leaves the building in a soft grey briefcase, and with it the only proof that procedure was followed by a ghost. The night stamper starts a fresh sheet without a word, and dates it, and the date sits there like a small accusation you can spend.

choices.text: Rebuild the card from four hundred memories

success.text: The patrons who sheltered the catalog return carrying what patrons always keep: details. A retired schoolmistress reproduces the fee's compounding column from memory, two clerks certify the ink color against a swatch, and the night stamper strikes a fresh card to the disputed die's exact measurements. Twelve witnesses initial the reconstruction, and the deputy head of Returns rules it a true copy, admissible, beloved.
effects: schedule 3 r18_public_library_compromise The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

fail.text: Memory, it turns out, is a lending system with heavy losses. Four hundred patrons recall six hundred different fees, one of them in a currency that has never existed, and the reconciliation meeting ends with a thrown atlas and your eyebrow needing tape. The reconstruction is filed under Disputed, which in this building is a shallow grave.
effects: schedule 3 r18_public_library_compromise The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

text:
PUBLIC LIBRARY

The settlement arrives by liveried courier: Knowledge Access Premium will forgive the ancient fee, fund a commemorative plaque, and endow a reading nook, provided the library certifies one particular book as lost beyond recall. The document runs sixty pages, and page fifty three is where the archivist emeritus stops turning and starts smiling. Certifying a book lost, she notes, requires the fee to exist, and they have just spent fifty two pages describing an account they publicly closed. The deputy head of Returns proposes, in reply, something the lending code has not seen in nine hundred years: an amnesty.

choices.text: Read all sixty pages, appendices included

success.text: Appendix J waives the library's archival handling costs, which means somebody calculated them, which means somebody owes them. You invoice Knowledge Access Premium for eight centuries of storing their problem, at the standard rate, and they pay four gold rather than argue the arithmetic in public. The brigade is uneasy about touching their coin at all, but the deputy head of Returns rules that money accepted under a fee schedule is not a gift, it is a fine, and fines are the house specialty.
effects: schedule 4 r18_public_library_reversal The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

fail.text: Somewhere around appendix R the language goes warm and the numbers go large, and you sign the consultancy addendum before quite finishing the sentence it lives in. Eight gold, payable immediately, for advisory services rendered to the settlement process, which is you, advising them, apparently. The brigade reads the addendum on the notice board where the account manager has helpfully posted it, your signature reproduced at a flattering size.
effects: schedule 4 r18_public_library_reversal The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

choices.text: Declare amnesty week: all debts home, no questions

outcomes.text: The notice goes up in every branch and on the bookmobile's flank: one week, all fines forgiven, all returns welcome, no questions asked or permitted. Books start coming home within the hour, some wrapped in aprons, some in apology notes, one strapped to a pigeon rated for hardback. The night stamper cancels fee after fee with the big stamp, two-handed, like a man ringing a bell.
effects: schedule 4 r18_public_library_reversal The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

outcomes.text: Amnesty week opens to a line around the block, which is when you learn that forgiveness has a load-bearing limit. On day two the returns bin avalanches, and you are under it, briefly, cradling a nine-hundred-year-old bestiary the way you were trained, spine supported, yours less so. The line applauds, the bin is promoted to a trough, and the healers bill the kitty.
effects: schedule 4 r18_public_library_reversal The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

choices.text: Sign their settlement, amended in the margins

success.text: You accept the settlement with three marginal amendments in archival pencil, initialed, tiny: lost beyond recall becomes not currently shelved, forgive becomes acknowledge, and the plaque's wording is now chosen by the library. Their counsel signs the pile at speed, the way people sign what they believe they wrote. Knowledge Access Premium has now acknowledged, in triplicate, an account it swears was never real, and the plaque will say so in brass.
effects: schedule 4 r18_public_library_reversal The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

fail.text: Their counsel, it develops, reads margins for a living. The amendments are struck, the meeting is minuted, and the minutes describe the library's negotiating posture as creative, a word lawyers use the way coroners use interesting. The settlement dies on the table, which was your fallback, except the fallback did not include the account manager looking, for one honest second, relieved.
effects: schedule 4 r18_public_library_reversal The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

text:
PUBLIC LIBRARY

Under the good lamp, at last, the archivist emeritus lays the reconstructed card beside a certified rubbing of the kingdom's founding charter, and the room stops breathing. The hands match, loop for loop, the smudge resolving into the first signature anyone here was taught to trust. Nine days before the founding, the founder borrowed An Honest Map of Who Owned What, and a kingdom is considerably easier to establish when nobody can check. The fee is older than the kingdom because the theft is, and the deputy head of Returns says the only thing she knows how to say: the account is still active.

choices.text: Fund a public reading of the evidence, candles lit

outcomes.text: You cover the candles, the extra chairs, and the schoolmistress's throat lozenges, and the reading runs past midnight to a room that will not thin. Nobody editorializes; the brigade simply reads the record aloud, dates, sums, interest table twelve, and lets eight centuries of arithmetic do its own talking. Patrons file out in the particular silence of people recalculating a very old sum. The candle stubs are archived, labeled, because tonight of all nights, chain of custody.
effects: schedule 5 r18_public_library_legacy The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

outcomes.text: The reading draws a crowd, and crowds draw the kind of men who attend history with clubs. You take one across the shoulder covering the schoolmistress's exit, the candles go over, and the evening ends behind a wall of returns carts while the room empties. The record survives, your shoulder files a complaint, and the street stays loud until morning.
effects: schedule 5 r18_public_library_legacy The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

choices.text: Enter the finding as a routine catalog correction

outcomes.text: No proclamation, no pamphlet: just an errata slip in the public catalog, cross-referenced, stating that the borrower of record for one overdue volume is the founding signature of the kingdom, see charter, see fee, see appendix. It is the quietest sentence in the building and the loudest thing the kingdom has read in centuries. The registry, bound by its own rules, must index it, and does, hating every minute.
effects: schedule 5 r18_public_library_legacy The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

outcomes.text: The catalog correction is impounded within the day under an emergency statute concerning heritage stability, a phrase nobody had needed before lunch. The errata slip is now itself overdue, having been removed without a date stamp, which the deputy head of Returns enters in the loss register with grim satisfaction. Being right, the archivist emeritus observes, is a reference service, not a shield.
effects: schedule 5 r18_public_library_legacy The fee is still compounding. The library remembers.

choices.text: Sell the charter comparison to a foreign broadsheet

outcomes.text: The broadsheet pays in advance and prints in red. By week's end the founder's signature is a tavern song in two duchies and your purse is heavier than your name is clean. Knowledge Access Premium, curiously, buys out the entire second printing, which does not stop the song, songs being the one format they cannot recall. The brigade never asks where your new boots came from, which is its own kind of invoice.

text:
PUBLIC LIBRARY

Amnesty week ends on a Sunday, and Monday's returns bin holds one parcel wrapped in oilcloth, tied with cord, no note. Inside is An Honest Map of Who Owned What, complete, cool to the touch, smelling faintly of nine days before the kingdom. Beside it lies the original fee, the stolen card itself, stamped PAID IN FULL in a hand nobody recognizes and an ink the archivist emeritus dates twice because she does not believe herself. The night stamper checks the master stamp, dry, locked, untouched, and reports the blank after-hours sheet in the voice of a man deciding to be religious about it.

choices.text: Catalog the book so it can never be uncataloged

success.text: You shelve it in the public stacks under a call number cross-referenced into every index the building owns: maps, history, law, fiction for safety, and the errata. Removing it now would require unwinding the entire catalog, a task the bindery estimates at four hundred years, fee schedule available on request. Knowledge Access Premium sends one final letter, and the deputy head of Returns stamps it RECEIVED, files it under Correspondence, Concluded, and goes to lunch.

fail.text: Your cross-referencing is ambitious, and ambition in a catalog is how ghosts get made: the book now appears in eleven indexes under nine call numbers, two of which contradict. Scholars will feud over the true shelf for generations, which, the archivist emeritus points out, at least guarantees the feuding happens here, in public, for free. The registry cites the library for index irregularities, the fine is one copper, and the kitty pays it laughing.

choices.text: Charge pilgrims a copper to view the paid card

outcomes.text: They come from three duchies to see the stamp, and a copper a look adds up the way interest always promised it would. The card sits under glass, framed above the returns desk, out-earning most clerks. Nobody can prove the viewing fee is wrong, exactly, and the deputy head of Returns says only that the card spent eight centuries working for the public and you have given it a second career. You tell yourself it would want that, and the card, being paid in full, declines to comment.
